originalEn.meaning_en: It means the realistic wisdom of overcoming a crisis by negotiating with external forces while enduring humiliation amidst a national crisis.

originalEn.quote_en: Patient effort to fill the gaps and stand up cracked pillars extends the lifespan of a crumbling empire.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows the patience of taking over a country on the verge of collapse from enemies on all sides and overcoming crises step by step.

originalEn.quote_en: Sometimes one hoists internal sails by borrowing a great external wind, but it is governance to steer so that the wind does not swallow the ship.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the insight of struggling not to lose the initiative while preventing immediate threats by drawing in foreign armies.

originalEn.event: Born in Constantinople.
Ascends to the throne after a successful coup, beginning his reign as Byzantine Emperor.
Issues a Golden Bull granting extensive trading privileges to the Venetians in exchange for naval support against the Normans.
Decisively defeats the Pechenegs at the Battle of Levounion, ending their threat to Constantinople.
Sends envoys to Pope Urban II at the Council of Piacenza, requesting military aid against the Seljuk Turks.
The armies of the First Crusade arrive at Constantinople; Alexios secures oaths of fealty from their leaders.
Byzantine forces, in cooperation with the Crusaders, recapture Nicaea, Smyrna, Ephesus, and other key Anatolian territories.
Dies in Constantinople, leaving a stabilized and recovering empire to his son, John II Komnenos.

originalEn.quote_en: Do not try to know everything in the world, or you will remain ignorant.
originalEn.meaning_en: This means that true wisdom comes not from knowing everything, but from delving deeply into one field. It emphasizes the importance of depth of knowledge.

originalEn.quote_en: If there were no power in speech, could the world be conquered by a stroke of the pen?
originalEn.meaning_en: This statement asserts the immense power of language and literature. It reflects the belief that the pen is mightier than the sword, and that well-crafted words have the power to build and topple empires.

originalEn.quote_en: It is his knowledge that makes a man a man.
originalEn.meaning_en: This means that a person's worth lies not in wealth or status, but in the knowledge and wisdom they have accumulated. It highlights the importance of learning and self-reflection.

originalEn.event: Born in Herat, then a major city in the Timurid Empire (present-day Afghanistan).
Forced to flee Herat with his family after the death of the Timurid ruler Shahrukh.
Returns to Herat after his childhood friend, Husayn Bayqara, becomes the ruler. He is appointed as a high-ranking official and advisor.
Appointed as vizier (chief minister), gaining significant political power and influence.
Composes his magnum opus, the 'Khamsa' (Quintet), a collection of five epic poems in the Chagatai language.
Completes 'Muhakamat al-Lughatayn' (The Comparison of the Two Languages), a treatise arguing for the literary superiority of Turkic over Persian.
Dies in Herat, leaving behind a vast legacy of literary works and architectural patronage.

originalEn.quote_en: The most superior among you (Muslims) are those who learn the Qur'an and teach it.
originalEn.meaning_en: Emphasizes the supreme importance of acquiring and disseminating sacred knowledge.

originalEn.quote_en: Knowledge is that which benefits, not that which is memorized.
originalEn.meaning_en: True knowledge is not just rote memorization but practical wisdom that positively impacts one's life and the lives of others.

originalEn.quote_en: I have not included in my book 'Sahih' but what is authentic.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his unwavering commitment to accuracy, intellectual honesty, and the rigorous methodology that defined his life's work.

originalEn.event: Born in Bukhara, Transoxiana (present-day Uzbekistan).
Reportedly lost his sight in early childhood but miraculously regained it.
At age 16, he traveled to Mecca for the Hajj pilgrimage and began his extensive journeys to collect hadith.
Spent over 16 years traveling throughout the Abbasid Caliphate, including major centers of learning in Iraq, Syria, Egypt, and the Hejaz.
Completed his magnum opus, 'Sahih al-Bukhari', after meticulously sifting through over 600,000 narrations.
Faced a theological dispute in Nishapur, leading to his exile.
Died in Khartank, a village near Samarkand, after being exiled from his hometown of Bukhara.

originalEn.quote_en: The stars do not lie. Observe them diligently, and the universe will reveal its elegant structure.
originalEn.meaning_en: Emphasizes the belief that accurate and persistent observation is the key to understanding the fundamental truths of the natural world.

originalEn.quote_en: True mastery is not in holding complex knowledge, but in making it clear for all to understand.
originalEn.meaning_en: Showcases his philosophy as an educator: the value of knowledge lies in the ability to popularize and teach it, not in its exclusivity.

originalEn.quote_en: The study of the cosmos is not separate from faith; it is a path to appreciating the grandeur of the Creator's work.
originalEn.meaning_en: Reflects the common view of Islamic scholars of his time, where scientific inquiry was seen as a means to deepen religious awe.

originalEn.event: Born in the Fergana Valley, Transoxiana (modern-day Uzbekistan).
Moved to Baghdad and began working at the House of Wisdom under the patronage of Caliph al-Ma'mun.
Wrote his most famous work, 'Elements of Astronomy' (Jawāmiʿ ʿIlm al-Nujūm).
Participated in the project to measure the diameter of the Earth in the Syrian desert.
Traveled to Egypt to design and supervise the construction of the Great Nilometer on Rawda Island, Fustat (Old Cairo).
His exact death year is unknown, but his last recorded activity was in 861.

originalEn.quote_en: If you live in hatred, in quarrels and fighting, you will perish yourselves and ruin the land of your fathers and grandfathers, which they had created with their great labor.
originalEn.meaning_en: Yaroslav's famous admonition to his sons on his deathbed. It reflects the lessons learned from his own life of fraternal conflict and emphasizes the importance of unity for the survival of the state.

originalEn.quote_en: A book is a river that makes the universe flow with its wisdom.
originalEn.meaning_en: Often attributed to him or the chronicles of his era, this quote highlights his dedication to learning, books, and the establishment of libraries. He saw knowledge as key to the nation's development.

originalEn.quote_en: If a man kills a man, then the brother is to avenge his brother, or the son his father, or the father his son...
originalEn.meaning_en: This excerpt from the Russkaya Pravda represents his core achievement: establishing a codified system of law that began to replace arbitrary blood feuds with structured justice and fines.

originalEn.event: 블라디미르 대공의 아들로 태어남.
블라디미르 대공 사망 후, 형제간의 왕위 계승 전쟁 시작.
알타 강 전투에서 형 스뱌토폴크를 물리치고 키예프 대공이 됨.
최초의 루스 법전인 '루스카야 프라브다'의 초기 버전을 제정함.
키예프 공성전에서 페체네그족을 결정적으로 격파하고 루스의 유일한 통치자가 됨.
승리를 기념하여 키예프에 성 소피아 대성당 건설을 시작함.
다섯 아들에게 영토를 나누어준 후 비슈호로드에서 사망함.

originalEn.quote_en: Opportunity does not come to those who wait, but to those who row against the current.
originalEn.meaning_en: This means that true opportunities are seized not by waiting passively, but by actively moving against the unknown risks and difficulties.

originalEn.quote_en: A handful of brave warriors is better than ten thousand cowards.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects the belief that the outcome of a war depends not on the number of soldiers, but on their courage and morale. It emphasizes the power of a small, elite force.

originalEn.quote_en: My sword is for the Tsar, my soul is for God, and my heart is for Russia.
originalEn.meaning_en: This statement shows that his actions were based on a greater loyalty to his country, monarch, and faith, rather than for personal glory.

originalEn.event: Born, likely in a village on the Northern Dvina River. His early life is largely undocumented.
Gained notoriety as a Cossack ataman, possibly engaging in piracy and mercenary activities along the Volga and Don rivers.
Hired by the Stroganov merchant family, Yermak leads a force of over 800 Cossacks across the Ural Mountains to confront the Khanate of Sibir.
Achieves a decisive victory against Khan Kuchum's forces at the Battle of Chuvash Cape.
Captures Qashliq (Isker), the capital of the Khanate of Sibir, forcing Khan Kuchum to flee into the steppes.
Yermak sends his lieutenant Ivan Koltso to Moscow with news of the conquest and a tribute of furs. Tsar Ivan the Terrible officially pardons the Cossacks and sends reinforcements.
Ambushed by Khan Kuchum's forces while camping on an island in the Irtysh River. Yermak drowns, allegedly weighed down by the chainmail armor gifted to him by the Tsar.

originalEn.quote_en: The bee is more honored than other animals, not because she labors, but because she labors for others.
originalEn.meaning_en: This means the true value of labor lies in its service to the community and others. Altruistic dedication, not selfish ambition, earns respect.

originalEn.quote_en: No matter how just your words may be, you ruin everything when you speak with anger.
originalEn.meaning_en: The manner of delivery is as important as the message itself. Anger undermines the power of truth and hinders communication.

originalEn.quote_en: To be right with God has often meant to be in trouble with men.
originalEn.meaning_en: This shows that upholding divine principles and truth can lead to conflict with worldly powers or popular opinion. It takes courage to stand by one's convictions.

originalEn.event: Born in Antioch to a noble family.
Begins an ascetic life as a hermit in the mountains near Antioch after his mother's death.
Ordained a presbyter (priest) in Antioch and begins his celebrated preaching career.
Consecrated Archbishop of Constantinople against his will, becoming a major figure in the imperial capital.
Condemned and briefly exiled by the Synod of the Oak, orchestrated by his political and ecclesiastical enemies.
Exiled a second time by Empress Eudoxia, this time to Cucusus in Armenia.
Dies during a forced march to a more remote place of exile in Pityus on the Black Sea.

originalEn.quote_en: It is a noble passion that devoted a lifetime to seeking the truth of the night sky rather than enjoying worldly glory at the peak of power.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means a life that contributed to the development of astronomy by placing greater value on the pursuit of scientific truth than the lust for power as a ruler.

originalEn.quote_en: The borders of an empire made by a sword will eventually crumble, but the monumental tower of knowledge built by learning endures a thousand years.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows that the legacy left through precise astronomical observation and encouragement of learning is more deeply engraved in human history than military achievements.

originalEn.quote_en: Even the outstanding wisdom that reads the logic of the sky cannot fathom the foolish greed and betrayal of the human world.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the reality of meeting a tragic end due to a blood relative's secret strife for power, despite seeing through the order of the universe.

originalEn.event: Born Mīrzā Muhammad Tāraghay bin Shāhrukh in Soltaniyeh, Persia.
Appointed as the governor of Samarkand at the age of 16 by his father, Shah Rukh.
Begins construction of the Ulugh Beg Madrasa (an Islamic school) on the Registan square in Samarkand.
Builds the great Ulugh Beg Observatory in Samarkand to advance the study of astronomy.
Completes the 'Zij-i Sultani' (Sultanic Star Tables), a monumental star catalogue and astronomical treatise.
Ascends to the throne of the Timurid Empire after his father Shah Rukh's death, leading to a period of instability.
Defeated in battle by his rebellious son, Abd al-Latif Mirza, and subsequently assassinated near Samarkand.

originalEn.quote_en: Justice is the constant and perpetual will to render to each his due.
originalEn.meaning_en: This was the core principle of his legal codification. The goal of law is to ensure what is fair and just for every person, which is the foundation of a stable society.

originalEn.quote_en: The safety of the State is the highest law.
originalEn.meaning_en: This Roman legal maxim summarizes his belief that the well-being and security of the empire must take precedence over all other considerations, justifying his sometimes ruthless political decisions.

originalEn.quote_en: One God, one Empire, one Religion.
originalEn.meaning_en: This motto reflects his governing philosophy. He envisioned a unified Christian Roman Empire where religious orthodoxy and imperial authority were inextricably linked.

originalEn.event: Birth of Flavius Petrus Sabbatius in Tauresium.
Ascends to the throne as Emperor Justinian I.
The Nika riots erupt in Constantinople and are brutally suppressed.
The Corpus Juris Civilis (Body of Civil Law) is compiled and published.
General Belisarius reconquers North Africa from the Vandals.
The rebuilt Hagia Sophia is consecrated in Constantinople.
The Plague of Justinian begins, a pandemic that devastates the empire's population.
The Gothic War concludes, with Byzantium nominally controlling Italy after a long and destructive conflict.
Death of Justinian I in Constantinople.

originalEn.quote_en: A sovereign answers to no one but God.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects my ideology of autocracy and sovereignty, that the power of the monarch is divine and not subject to any earthly authority.

originalEn.quote_en: It is better to acquire lands through purchase and inheritance than through the sword, but the sword must always be ready.
originalEn.meaning_en: This demonstrates my pragmatic approach to expansion; I preferred peaceful means but did not hesitate to use military force for the benefit of the state.

originalEn.quote_en: A divided house cannot stand against the Horde. Unity is our only shield and our sharpest spear.
originalEn.meaning_en: This was my core governing philosophy. I believed that unifying the Rus' principalities under Moscow was the only way to secure independence and survival against external threats.

originalEn.event: Born in Moscow to Grand Prince Vasily II.
Ascends to the throne as Grand Prince of Moscow.
Defeats the army of the Novgorod Republic at the Battle of Shelon River.
Formally annexes the Republic of Novgorod, ending its independence.
The Great Stand on the Ugra River, which marks the de facto end of the Mongol-Tatar yoke over Russia.
Annexes the Principality of Tver, his last major Russian rival.
Issues the Sudebnik, a new code of laws for the unified state.
Dies in Moscow, leaving a vastly expanded and centralized state to his son, Vasily III.

originalEn.quote_en: I have been a ruler for a long time, and I have always tried to do what is best for my people.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights the internal justification many absolute rulers use, believing their actions, however harsh, are for the greater good of the state and its people.

originalEn.quote_en: The state is not for the people, the people are for the state.
originalEn.meaning_en: This quote encapsulates the core principle of absolute monarchy, where the individual's purpose is to serve the needs and glory of the nation, as embodied by the monarch.

originalEn.quote_en: Treason is like a snake; it is better to crush its head before it has a chance to strike.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reveals the mindset behind his brutal purges, particularly the Oprichnina, viewing extreme measures as necessary precautions against betrayal.

originalEn.event: Becomes Grand Prince of Moscow at age three.
Crowned as the first 'Tsar of All Russia'.
Conquers the Khanate of Kazan.
Conquers the Khanate of Astrakhan.
His wife, Anastasia Romanovna, dies, marking a turn in his personality.
Establishes the Oprichnina, a period of mass repression.
Leads the Massacre of Novgorod.
Kills his eldest son and heir, Ivan Ivanovich, in a fit of rage.
Dies, leaving the throne to his younger, unfit son Feodor.

originalEn.quote_en: I undertook all this for my homeland, for unfortunate Ukraine.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes that my actions stemmed not from personal ambition, but from a patriotic desire to liberate my oppressed homeland.

originalEn.quote_en: It is better to die in glory than to live in shame.
originalEn.meaning_en: This statement shows my resolve. It reflects the belief that it is more worthwhile to risk one's life for the cause of freedom than to live a life of subjugation.

originalEn.quote_en: Time will reveal the truth of my intentions.
originalEn.meaning_en: This expresses a belief that although I was branded a 'traitor' in my time, my true motivations and legacy would eventually be judged correctly by posterity.

originalEn.event: Born in Mazepyntsi, near Bila Tserkva, then part of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth.
Elected Hetman of the Zaporizhian Host of Left-bank Ukraine.
Ruled as Hetman, initially as a loyal vassal to Tsar Peter the Great of Russia, and became a great patron of arts and culture.
Briefly united both Left-bank and Right-bank Ukraine under his rule during an uprising against Poland.
Secretly allied with Charles XII of Sweden against Peter the Great during the Great Northern War.
Suffered a decisive defeat alongside the Swedes at the Battle of Poltava.
Died in exile in Bendery (Tighina), then part of the Ottoman Empire.

originalEn.quote_en: Our goal is human happiness, freedom, and brotherhood.
originalEn.meaning_en: This expresses the core of his socialist and humanist beliefs, emphasizing that all political and artistic activities should ultimately aim for universal human values.

originalEn.quote_en: He who has not made a single person happy is not worthy of the name of man.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes the impact of individual actions on others and society, stressing the importance of social good and contribution to others.

originalEn.quote_en: A book is a ship of thought, wandering through the waves of time and carefully carrying its precious cargo from generation to generation.
originalEn.meaning_en: This shows his belief in the eternal value of knowledge and literature as vessels that transcend time to carry human wisdom across generations.

originalEn.event: Born in Nahuievychi, Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria (modern-day Ukraine).
Enrolls at Lviv University and begins publishing his first literary works.
First arrested for socialist activities and belonging to a secret society.
Co-founds the Ruthenian-Ukrainian Radical Party, the first modern Ukrainian political party.
Defends his doctoral dissertation, 'Barlaam and Josaphat', at the University of Vienna.
Publishes his philosophical epic poem 'Moses' (Мойсей), a cornerstone of his literary legacy.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Dies in Lviv at the age of 59, impoverished but revered as a national icon.

originalEn.quote_en: Dilde, fikirde, işte birlik (Unity in language, thought, and action).
originalEn.meaning_en: His core philosophy that a shared, simplified Turkic language would foster common understanding and unified action, leading to progress and modernization for all Turkic peoples.

originalEn.quote_en: Our ignorance is the main reason for our backward condition. We have no access at all to what has been discovered and to what is going on in Europe.
originalEn.meaning_en: A direct call to action, identifying lack of modern knowledge as the root cause of his people's decline and stressing the need to embrace European science and thought.

originalEn.quote_en: The newspaper is the people's tongue, eye, and ear.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights his belief in the power of the press to educate, inform, and unite a scattered populace, making Terciman the central tool of his reform movement.

originalEn.event: Born in Avcıköy, Crimea, Russian Empire.
Travels to Paris, where he is influenced by French liberal thought and socialist ideas.
First attempts to get permission from the Russian government to publish a newspaper in a Turkic language, but is denied.
Successfully founds the newspaper Terciman (The Interpreter) in Bakhchysarai, which becomes a voice for Turkic intellectuals.
Establishes a 'New Method' (Usul-i Jadid) school in Bakhchysarai, pioneering his educational reforms.
Elected chairman of the First All-Russian Muslim Congress, a major step in organizing the political activities of Muslims in the empire.
Dies in Bakhchysarai, Crimea, leaving a legacy of reform and enlightenment.

originalEn.quote_en: An error in calculation, no matter how small, can lead a traveler astray by leagues. Thus, we must pursue truth with the utmost precision.
originalEn.meaning_en: Emphasizes that accuracy and precision are fundamental to scientific inquiry, as even minor mistakes can lead to significant errors.

originalEn.quote_en: The heavens do not lie. It is our theories that must bend to the evidence written in the stars, not the other way around.
originalEn.meaning_en: Illustrates the core of the scientific method: observation and empirical evidence must take precedence over preconceived theories.

originalEn.quote_en: Knowledge is a garden. If it is not cultivated, it will be overgrown with the weeds of ignorance.
originalEn.meaning_en: A metaphor for the idea that knowledge requires constant effort and learning to be maintained and expanded.

originalEn.event: Born in Kashan, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Observed a lunar eclipse in Kashan, which he later used for his calculations.
Completed his work 'Sullam al-Sama' ('The Stairway of Heaven') on the sizes and distances of celestial bodies.
Was invited by Prince Ulugh Beg to join the scientific center in Samarkand.
Completed 'The Treatise on the Circumference,' calculating 2π to 9 sexagesimal digits, equivalent to 16 decimal places.
Completed his magnum opus, 'Miftah al-Hisab' ('The Key to Arithmetic'), which explains the use of decimal fractions.
Died in Samarkand, under circumstances that are still debated by historians.

originalEn.quote_en: The spirit of a people lives in its historical legends, tales, and songs.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes that a people's identity and values are deeply rooted not just in official records, but in the oral heritage passed down through generations.

originalEn.quote_en: An educated mind is a lamp that illuminates the darkest night of ignorance.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights the importance of enlightenment and knowledge, expressing the belief that education allows individuals and societies to progress and overcome prejudice.

originalEn.quote_en: To understand the world, one must first know the ground beneath one's own feet.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes that before becoming a global scholar, the starting point of all learning is to deeply study one's own roots—the history and culture of one's own land.

originalEn.event: Born as Muhammed Qanapiya in the Kushmurun fortress, Kostanay Region.
Enrolled in the Omsk Cadet Corps, a Russian military academy.
Graduated from the Cadet Corps and began his military service as an aide-de-camp to the Governor-General of Western Siberia.
Undertook his first major scholarly expedition to the Issyk-Kul region, where he recorded parts of the Epic of Manas.
Conducted a perilous secret mission to Kashgar, disguised as a merchant, gathering crucial ethnographic and intelligence data.
Worked in St. Petersburg at the Foreign Office and the Russian Geographical Society, becoming a celebrated intellectual figure.
Died from tuberculosis at the age of 29 in the Altyn-Emel region.

originalEn.quote_en: Beauty merely pleases the eye, but sharp intellect pierces through the world and stands against the prejudice of the times.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means the attitude of rejecting evaluation based on flashy appearance and proudly standing against arrogant power with one's own insight and wisdom.

originalEn.quote_en: Only one who can refuse a splendid seat permitted by the world can enjoy freedom of the soul and sing an eternal song.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows an independent life devoted to religion and art, rather than considering not being chosen by a person of power as a failure.

originalEn.quote_en: A suppressed voice puts on the wings of poetry and music, crosses the prison walls, and revives as an echo of a thousand years.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the footprint of leaving one's own truth through outstanding composition and poetry creation in an era when it was difficult for women to speak up.

originalEn.event: Born into a wealthy family in Constantinople.
Participated in the bride-show for Emperor Theophilos, where her famous witty exchange took place.
Founded a convent in Constantinople and became its first abbess, following the end of the second Iconoclast period.
Died. The exact year of her death is unknown.
Venerated as a saint in the Eastern Orthodox Church. Her feast day is celebrated on September 7th.

originalEn.quote_en: To surrender the city to you is beyond my authority or that of any of its citizens. We have all decided to die with our own free will.
originalEn.meaning_en: A declaration of ultimate duty and defiance, prioritizing the collective will and honor of the city over personal survival or the authority of a single ruler.

originalEn.quote_en: I have a great many of my nobles, and they have sworn to die with me.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights the shared sacrifice and loyalty between the emperor and his people in the face of annihilation, emphasizing unity in the final moments.

originalEn.quote_en: The world has seen many cities fall, but never one like this.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects the understanding that the fall of Constantinople was not just a military defeat, but a world-historical event, the end of a civilization and a symbol of Christendom's last bastion in the East.

originalEn.event: Born in Constantinople to Emperor Manuel II Palaiologos and Helena Dragaš.
Becomes co-Despot of the Morea, a Byzantine province in the Peloponnese, with his brothers.
Ascends to the Byzantine throne as Constantine XI Palaiologos following the death of his brother, John VIII.
Mehmed II becomes the Ottoman Sultan and begins preparations for the conquest of Constantinople.
The final siege of Constantinople by the Ottoman Empire begins.
Dies in battle during the final assault as the city falls to the Ottomans, marking the end of the Byzantine Empire.

originalEn.quote_en: I have conquered the world on horseback, but I cannot rule it from horseback.
originalEn.meaning_en: This acknowledges that the skills needed for conquest are different from those needed for governance. It shows an understanding that to maintain an empire, stable administration and cultural understanding are as crucial as military might.

originalEn.quote_en: The foundation of a state is its people.
originalEn.meaning_en: This Confucian sentiment shows my understanding as a ruler that the well-being and support of the populace are essential for the stability of the empire. It symbolizes my transformation from a conqueror to a governor.

originalEn.quote_en: There is a unity in the world, if only one has the vision to see it.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects my ambition to build an empire that encompassed diverse cultures, religions, and peoples. I sought to build a bridge between East and West through the free exchange of goods and ideas.

originalEn.event: Born as the grandson of Genghis Khan.
Proclaimed Great Khan of the Mongol Empire after defeating his brother Ariq Böke.
Officially established the Yuan dynasty and proclaimed himself Emperor of China.
Marco Polo allegedly arrives at Kublai Khan's court in Dadu (Beijing).
Completed the conquest of the Southern Song dynasty, unifying all of China under his rule.
Launched two naval invasions of Japan, both of which failed, partly due to major storms.
Died in Dadu at the age of 78.

originalEn.quote_en: To what a barbarous country have you sent me...
originalEn.meaning_en: This quote highlights the stark cultural differences between the sophisticated court of Kievan Rus' and early Capetian France, and the value of education and refinement.

originalEn.quote_en: Anna Regina
originalEn.meaning_en: This signature, often appearing alongside the king's, signifies her unusual level of political power and literacy for a queen of her time, asserting her authority and active role in governance.

originalEn.quote_en: A bridge is built not of stone, but of understanding.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects her life's role as a connection between two disparate cultures, emphasizing that true connection comes from shared knowledge and mutual respect, not just political alliances.

originalEn.event: Born in Kyiv, daughter of Grand Prince Yaroslav the Wise of Kievan Rus'.
Marries King Henry I of France in Reims Cathedral, becoming Queen of the Franks.
Gives birth to her first son and future king, Philip I.
King Henry I dies. Anne serves as regent for the eight-year-old Philip I.
Marries Count Ralph IV of Valois, causing a scandal and her temporary excommunication.
Last recorded signature on a document under her son Philip's reign. Her exact death date is unknown.

originalEn.quote_en: Justice must sometimes be cold and calculated. Emotion only dulls the blade of vengeance.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects that her revenge was not merely an emotional outburst, but a series of political acts designed to consolidate power and establish order.

originalEn.quote_en: True rule comes not from the sword, but from order. It is more important to build roads than to simply collect taxes.
originalEn.meaning_en: This signifies her shift from vengeance to governance, focusing on administrative reforms like the 'pogosti' system, which shows her vision for long-term state stability.

originalEn.quote_en: I saw a light in the darkness for my people. Though it did not reach my son, it will reach my grandson.
originalEn.meaning_en: Her conversion to Christianity and efforts to spread it were not immediately successful, but they bore fruit with her grandson, Vladimir the Great. This symbolizes her pioneering spirit and foresight.

originalEn.event: Born in Pskov (approximate date) and later married Igor of Kyiv.
Igor of Kyiv is murdered by the Drevlians. Olga becomes regent for her young son, Sviatoslav.
Carries out a four-part, systematic revenge against the Drevlians, culminating in the burning of their capital, Iskorosten.
Institutes administrative reforms, establishing a network of 'pogosti' (administrative centers) for systematic tax collection.
Travels to Constantinople and is baptized into Orthodox Christianity, taking the Christian name Elena (Helen).
Dies in Kyiv. She was later canonized as a saint by the Orthodox Church.

originalEn.quote_en: Fight, and you will overcome. God will help you!
originalEn.meaning_en: A powerful call to resist oppression, promising divine support for a just cause.

originalEn.quote_en: In the great family, The family new and free, Do not forget to remember me, With a kind, quiet word.
originalEn.meaning_en: A humble plea to be remembered by a future, free Ukraine, emphasizing the importance of memory and national unity.

originalEn.quote_en: In one's own house, there is one's own truth, And strength, and freedom.
originalEn.meaning_en: A powerful statement on national sovereignty, cultural self-reliance, and the importance of having one's own independent nation.

originalEn.event: Born into serfdom in the village of Moryntsi, Russian Empire.
Freed from serfdom through the efforts of Karl Bryullov and Vasily Zhukovsky.
Publishes his first and most famous collection of poetry, 'Kobzar'.
Arrested for his involvement in the political Brotherhood of Saints Cyril and Methodius and exiled.
Released from his ten-year military exile after the death of Tsar Nicholas I.
Dies in Saint Petersburg at the age of 47. He was later reburied in Ukraine.

originalEn.quote_en: One who endures the weight of the crown amidst prejudice turns oppression into tolerance and leads chaos into a peaceful golden age.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means the achievement of wisely overcoming the opposition and doubt experienced for being a female monarch and achieving the nation's greatest prosperity.

originalEn.quote_en: True power lies not in making others submit through fear, but in weaving hearts together through wise tuning and gentle harmony.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows outstanding leadership in persuading political opponents and binding various classes together instead of uncompromising purges.

originalEn.quote_en: A golden age is finally completed in a peaceful soil where learning and art can bloom, not just with material wealth.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the insight of enriching history by cultivating a brilliant culture comparable to a renaissance, without being satisfied with military victories.

originalEn.event: Tamar is born to King George III and Queen Burdukhan.
Appointed co-ruler and heir apparent by her father, George III.
Becomes the sole monarch of Georgia upon her father's death, taking the title 'Mepe' (King).
Is forced by the nobility to marry the Rus' prince Yuri Bogolyubsky.
Divorces and banishes Yuri due to his misconduct.
Chooses and marries the Alan prince David Soslan.
Georgian armies led by David Soslan achieve a major victory at the Battle of Shamkor.
Georgian forces win a decisive victory over the Seljuk Sultanate of Rûm at the Battle of Basian.
Helps establish the Empire of Trebizond on the Black Sea coast as a Georgian client state.
Tamar dies, leaving behind a powerful and prosperous kingdom. She is later canonized as a saint.

originalEn.quote_en: Royalty is a fine burial shroud.
originalEn.meaning_en: Spoken during the Nika Riots, this expresses my resolve that it is more honorable to die with the dignity of an emperor than to live as a fugitive. It symbolizes the courage and responsibility a leader must show in a crisis.

originalEn.quote_en: Those who have been wronged in their past deserve not only justice but also a path to redemption and dignity.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects my actions in establishing a convent for former prostitutes, offering them refuge and a new life. It speaks to my belief that all people, regardless of their past, deserve a second chance and the right to live with dignity.

originalEn.quote_en: Power is not given; it is seized by those with the will to hold it and the wisdom to wield it.
originalEn.meaning_en: This summarizes my life's journey from a humble background to the imperial throne. It signifies that destiny is not fixed but is forged by one's own will, ambition, and ability to act.

originalEn.event: Born in Constantinople or Cyprus, to a family of low social standing; her father was a bear keeper for the Hippodrome.
Meets Justinian, the heir to the throne, in Constantinople. She had been working as an actress and courtesan.
Marries Justinian. He had the law changed to permit their marriage, as it previously forbade officials from marrying actresses.
Crowned Augusta (Empress) alongside Justinian as Augustus (Emperor) of the Byzantine Empire.
Plays a pivotal role in convincing Justinian to stay and crush the Nika Riots, effectively saving his throne.
Enacts significant social reforms, including laws that prohibited forced prostitution and expanded women's rights in divorce and property ownership.
Dies, likely from cancer, in Constantinople. Justinian mourned her deeply and never remarried.

originalEn.quote_en: Light emanates not from the canvas, but from the artist's soul trying to face the truth in the darkness of a suffering world.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means the attitude of breaking formal molds and capturing religious depth and human spirituality on canvas with dynamic and intense expressions.

originalEn.quote_en: The true greatness of a master lies not in staying within one's own painting, but in opening a path for the talent of the next generation to stretch out.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the dedication of nurturing outstanding younger scholars in an unfamiliar land and inspiring the birth of great art different from his own.

originalEn.quote_en: Endless wandering to unfamiliar lands is a journey of truth-seeking, abandoning comfort and leaving only to find the completion of true art.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows an endless desire for art, leaving one's hometown and wandering in far-off foreign lands to complete one's own original painting style.

originalEn.event: Born in the Byzantine Empire.
Arrives in the Russian lands, first documented in Novgorod.
Paints the frescoes in the Church of the Transfiguration on Ilin Street, Novgorod.
Moves to Moscow and works on various commissions, including for the Grand Prince.
Works on the iconostasis of the Cathedral of the Annunciation in the Moscow Kremlin with Andrei Rublev and Prokhor of Gorodets.
Dies, likely in Moscow.

originalEn.quote_en: If a nation has a wise man at its head, it will not be without a ruler.
originalEn.meaning_en: Emphasizes that a nation's survival and prosperity depend on the wisdom and foresight of its leadership. Wise counsel and strategy are core to governance.

originalEn.quote_en: It is easy to stop a thin thing, it is easy to break a small thing. But if the thin becomes thick and the small becomes large, who can stop it?
originalEn.meaning_en: Highlights the importance of unity and strength. A small threat can become unstoppable if ignored, and conversely, a weak force can become invincible if united.

originalEn.quote_en: I, the wise Tonyukuk, was the one who had them led. As long as I, the wise Tonyukuk, am alive, the Khagan of the Turks and the Turkic people will be fortunate.
originalEn.meaning_en: Shows immense self-confidence and pride in his role and abilities. He believed himself to be indispensable to the survival and prosperity of the Turkic Khaganate.

originalEn.event: Born into the Ashina clan and later educated in the Tang capital of Chang'an.
Escapes from Tang control and persuades Qutlugh (later Ilterish Khagan) to revolt against the Tang Dynasty.
The Second Turkic Khaganate is founded; Tonyukuk is appointed as the chief strategist and commander (Apa Tarkan).
Ilterish Khagan dies. Tonyukuk continues to serve as the chief advisor under the new Khagan, Qapaghan.
After Qapaghan Khagan is killed, Tonyukuk plays a crucial role in supporting Bilge Khagan to secure the throne against a coup.
Composes his own memorial inscriptions, one of the oldest surviving examples of Turkic literature, written in the first person.
Dies at an advanced age, having served three Khagans and secured the foundation of the empire.

originalEn.quote_en: The whole expanse of the inhabited part of the world is not large enough to have two kings.
originalEn.meaning_en: This statement reflects Timur's ambition for absolute power and his vision of unifying the world under a single ruler. He believed that divided power only leads to conflict.

originalEn.quote_en: I ordered that the weak should not be oppressed, and that the powerful should not be pampered.
originalEn.meaning_en: Despite his brutal conquests, Timur projected an image of himself as a just ruler. This quote, from his purported memoirs, signifies his ideal of maintaining order and fairness within his empire.

originalEn.quote_en: Right-minded kings should not be satisfied with the countries of which they are in possession, but should ever be on the watch for the enterprise of conquest.
originalEn.meaning_en: This captures Timur's relentless expansionist mindset. For him, the duty of a ruler was not to maintain the status quo, but to constantly seek further territorial expansion.

originalEn.event: Born near Kesh (modern Shahrisabz, Uzbekistan) into the Barlas tribe.
Sustains severe injuries to his right leg and arm, earning him the nickname 'Timur the Lame'.
Becomes the sovereign ruler of the Chagatai Khanate's western territories and establishes Samarkand as his capital.
Invades India and sacks the city of Delhi, causing immense destruction.
Conquers Baghdad and Damascus, expanding his empire westward.
Defeats and captures the Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I at the Battle of Ankara.
Dies in Otrar while on a campaign to conquer Ming China.

originalEn.quote_en: The printing press is like the cannons of an army. Where it is well-aimed, it conquers; where it is not, it is conquered.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights the importance of education and the dissemination of knowledge. Mohyla saw the printing press as a powerful weapon to defend the Orthodox faith and enlighten the people.

originalEn.quote_en: We must borrow the learning of the West, but we must not sell our souls.
originalEn.meaning_en: This shows his reform philosophy: embrace advanced Western educational models while steadfastly preserving the unique identity and faith of Orthodoxy.

originalEn.quote_en: An ignorant faith is a house built on sand. Only an educated faith can stand upon the rock.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his belief that faith should not be blind, but must be supported by rational understanding and theological knowledge.

originalEn.event: Born in Suceava, Moldavia, to a noble Romanian family (Movilă).
Becomes a monk at the Kyiv Pechersk Lavra (Monastery of the Caves).
Elected Archimandrite (head abbot) of the Kyiv Pechersk Lavra.
Founds a school at the Lavra, emphasizing both theology and liberal arts.
Elected Metropolitan of Kyiv, Halych, and all Rus'. He merges his school with the Kyiv Brotherhood School to form the Kyiv-Mohyla Collegium (later Academy).
Convenes a council in Kyiv to discuss and approve his catechism, which would become the influential 'Orthodox Confession of Faith'.
Dies in Kyiv, leaving his fortune to the Collegium and cementing his legacy as a major reformer and educator.

originalEn.quote_en: Truly there would be reason to go mad were it not for music.
originalEn.meaning_en: Music was my only sanctuary and salvation, allowing me to maintain my sanity amidst a chaotic world and inner turmoil.

originalEn.quote_en: Inspiration is a guest that does not willingly visit the lazy.
originalEn.meaning_en: Great art is not born merely from a flash of inspiration, but must be earned through the price of constant effort and discipline.

originalEn.quote_en: To regret the past, to hope in the future, and never to be satisfied with the present: that is my life.
originalEn.meaning_en: My life was filled with constant internal conflict and dissatisfaction, but that very restlessness was the driving force that compelled me to keep moving forward and create new music.

originalEn.event: Born in Votkinsk, Russian Empire.
Studies at the newly founded Saint Petersburg Conservatory.
His Piano Concerto No. 1 premieres in Boston, becoming an instant success.
Premieres 'Swan Lake' to initial failure, enters a disastrous marriage, and begins his 13-year correspondence with his patron Nadezhda von Meck.
Composes his Fourth Symphony and the opera 'Eugene Onegin'.
The ballet 'The Sleeping Beauty' premieres. Nadezhda von Meck abruptly ends their relationship and patronage.
The ballet 'The Nutcracker' premieres in Saint Petersburg.
Conducts the premiere of his Symphony No. 6, the 'Pathétique', and dies nine days later.

originalEn.quote_en: Behind the flashy stage of history, there always hides the miserable truth and the bare face of corrupt power covered up by news of victory.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means the attitude of piercing through the hypocrisy and cruelty existing behind official praises and compliments, recording them separately, and leaving them for future generations.

originalEn.quote_en: The pen is the sharpest dagger recording the darkness of the times and the only judge that a person of power cannot control.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the power of intellect that drew a fair evaluation of history by secretly recording the truth at the risk of one's life even in an oppressive system.

originalEn.quote_en: One must remember the screams in the deep shadows as much as the praises pouring toward the bright light to see the whole face of history.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows a historical view that illuminated three-dimensionally not only the outward glory of the empire but also the suffering of the people sacrificed behind it.

originalEn.event: Born in Caesarea, Palaestina Prima.
Appointed as adsessor (legal advisor) to General Belisarius.
Accompanies Belisarius on the campaign against the Vandals in North Africa.
Participates in the Gothic War in Italy.
Witnesses the outbreak of the Plague of Justinian in Constantinople.
Completes his major work, The Wars of Justinian.
Likely writes the Secret History (Anecdota).
Writes On the Buildings of Justinian.
Dies, likely in Constantinople.

originalEn.quote_en: An Emperor must not command from a gilded throne, but from the saddle, sharing the hardships of his men.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects my belief that true leadership comes from being present with one's people and sharing in their struggles.

originalEn.quote_en: To cling to the past is to perish. An empire must change its tongue and its laws to match the spirit of its age.
originalEn.meaning_en: Practical reform and adaptation are essential for survival and prosperity, as shown by my change of the official language from Latin to Greek.

originalEn.quote_en: Victory is not won by the sword alone, but by the will of God who guides the righteous hand.
originalEn.meaning_en: This shows my deep religious faith, viewing the war against Persia as a holy crusade and believing all success was due to divine favor.

originalEn.event: Born in Cappadocia, son of the Exarch of Africa.
Overthrows the tyrannical Emperor Phocas and is proclaimed Emperor in Constantinople.
The Sassanid Persian army under Khosrow II captures Jerusalem and seizes the True Cross.
Personally leads a series of brilliant and daring military campaigns against the Sassanid Empire.
After defeating the Persians, recovers the True Cross and returns it to Jerusalem in a triumphal procession.
Makes Greek the official language of the Empire, replacing Latin, and adopts the title 'Basileus'.
The Byzantine army suffers a catastrophic defeat against the forces of the Rashidun Caliphate at the Battle of Yarmouk.
The provinces of Syria, Palestine, and Egypt are conquered by the advancing Arab armies.
Dies in Constantinople, having saved the empire from one foe only to see it dismembered by another.

originalEn.quote_en: A firm vow toward the truth is the most powerful compass that not even rough sandstorms and blizzards can stop.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means the unyielding spirit that achieved the true teachings of Buddhism by abandoning comfort and walking the path of seeking the law at the risk of his life.

originalEn.quote_en: Enlightenment is completed when it does not stay in one's own quiet rest but widely benefits the world by sharing what was learned with everyone.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the dedication of bringing massive scriptures from a distant foreign land, devoting his entire life to translation, and trying to pass on accurate wisdom to future generations.

originalEn.quote_en: Countless delusions and hardships experienced on the road are the tempering to wipe the vessel of the mind to approach ultimate truth.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows a journey that advanced toward a noble goal by overcoming his own limits without wavering, even in life-or-death crises and countless temptations.

originalEn.event: Born Chen Yi near Luoyang, Henan.
Fully ordained as a Buddhist monk.
Begins his journey to India, defying the Emperor's ban on travel.
Travels throughout India, studying at Nalanda University and collecting scriptures.
Returns to the Tang capital, Chang'an, with 657 Buddhist texts.
Completes his famous travelogue, 'Great Tang Records on the Western Regions'.
Leads a large state-sponsored translation project until his death.
Dies in Tongchuan, Shaanxi.

originalEn.quote_en: Know thyself.
originalEn.meaning_en: A core tenet of Skovoroda's philosophy, much like Socrates, is the belief that wisdom begins with understanding one's own inner world, not the external one.

originalEn.quote_en: We are born for happiness. Happiness is within us.
originalEn.meaning_en: This summarizes his teaching that happiness is not derived from external wealth or status, but is found in harmony with oneself and inner peace.

originalEn.quote_en: Cognate labor is the sweetest food.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights his concept of 'cognate labor' (srodna pratsia), the idea that true satisfaction and happiness come from performing work that aligns with one's nature and talents.

originalEn.event: Born in Chornukhy, Cossack Hetmanate, into a Cossack family.
Intermittently studied at the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy, excelling in languages, music, and philosophy.
Served as a singer in the Imperial Chapel in St. Petersburg.
Traveled through Europe as part of a mission, likely visiting Hungary, Vienna, and other centers of learning.
Taught poetics and ethics at Pereiaslav and Kharkiv Collegiums, but was dismissed due to his unorthodox methods and ideas.
Began his life as an itinerant philosopher, wandering through Ukraine, teaching, and writing.
Died in the village of Ivanivka (now Skovorodynivka), after reportedly digging his own grave the day before his death.

originalEn.quote_en: One can't be a great explorer and a good Christian.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects the internal conflict and compromises required when immersing oneself in different cultures and challenging the norms of one's own society.

originalEn.quote_en: The East is a university in which the scholar never takes his degree.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights her profound respect for the complexity and depth of Middle Eastern cultures, suggesting that there is always more to learn and understand.

originalEn.quote_en: There is no country in the world where it is so easy to make friends, so easy to be intimate, and so difficult to be intimate with the right people.
originalEn.meaning_en: This is a shrewd observation on the social and political landscape of the Middle East, noting the surface-level friendliness versus the difficulty of navigating complex power structures and allegiances.

originalEn.event: Born in Washington, County Durham, England.
Graduates from Oxford with a first-class honours degree in modern history.
Begins serious study of Arabic and makes her first major journey into the Syrian desert.
Recruited by British Intelligence at the outbreak of World War I.
Appointed Oriental Secretary to the British High Commissioner in Baghdad.
Plays a key role in the Cairo Conference, advocating for the creation of Iraq under King Faisal I.
Establishes the Baghdad Archaeological Museum (later the Iraq Museum).
Dies in Baghdad from an overdose of sleeping pills, believed to be suicide.

originalEn.quote_en: A kingdom cannot exist without a great army. And an army cannot be maintained without finances, and finances cannot be secured without the prosperity of the people.
originalEn.meaning_en: This statement reflects Nader Shah's pragmatic philosophy of governance, showing that while a nation's strength is based on military power, that power ultimately depends on a stable economy and the well-being of its people.

originalEn.quote_en: Destiny has no need of the aid of reason.
originalEn.meaning_en: This quote suggests his belief that his own incredible success could not be explained by logic or planning alone, but was driven by a greater force of destiny.

originalEn.quote_en: I have seen much, and I have learned this: a throne is but a piece of wood covered in velvet.
originalEn.meaning_en: After reaching the pinnacle of power, Nader Shah realized that the essence of the throne was not its glamour, but simply a seat, understanding its weight and ultimate emptiness.

originalEn.event: Born as Nader Qoli in the Khorasan province of Persia.
Enters the service of the Safavid claimant Tahmasp II and becomes his leading general.
Deposes the last Safavid ruler and is crowned Shah of Persia, founding the Afsharid dynasty.
Invades the Mughal Empire, defeats their army at the Battle of Karnal, and sacks the capital, Delhi.
Conquers Bukhara and Khiva, expanding the Persian empire to its greatest extent in the modern era.
Assassinated by a group of his own officers, leading to the rapid collapse of his empire.

originalEn.quote_en: The sharpest weapon to break the chains of oppression is not a blade, but the language of freedom that ignites a fire in people's hearts.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means the passion that criticized the country's reality and planted freedom and patriotism in the hearts of the people through literature and journalism rather than armed force.

originalEn.quote_en: Not even the pain of exile and prison can stop the voice of love for the homeland, and that cry resonates beyond the times.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the integrity of crying out for reform and liberation to the end without yielding, despite suffering continuous persecution and exile throughout his life.

originalEn.quote_en: One who awakens a dark era becomes firewood oneself for the light of tomorrow to come, even if they do not see immediate glory.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows the sacrifice and insight that laid the ideological foundation for the establishment of a new republic in the future, even though a complete goal was not achieved during his lifetime.

originalEn.event: Born in Tekirdağ, Ottoman Empire.
Joins the secret society of the Young Ottomans.
Flees to London and Paris, co-publishes the newspaper Hürriyet (Freedom).
Stages his play Vatan Yahut Silistre (Fatherland or Silistra), leading to his exile in Famagusta, Cyprus.
The first Ottoman Constitution is declared; Kemal serves on the drafting committee.
Sultan Abdülhamid II suspends the constitution and exiles Kemal again.
Dies in exile on the island of Chios.

originalEn.quote_en: Man is a civil being by nature and is social, seeking perfection through cooperation.
originalEn.meaning_en: A core idea from Tusi's 'Nasirean Ethics,' this quote emphasizes that humans cannot be whole in isolation and can only realize their potential through social cooperation.

originalEn.quote_en: That which is necessary to exist cannot be non-existent.
originalEn.meaning_en: A principle from his philosophical and logical writings, used to argue for the certainty of necessary truths, such as the existence of God or the fundamental laws of the universe.

originalEn.quote_en: The purpose of astronomy is to pose hypotheses based on the principle of uniform circular motion, which represent the actual motions of the celestial bodies.
originalEn.meaning_en: This statement summarizes his scientific goal: to critique Ptolemy's artificial 'equant' and create more elegant, physically plausible models, like the Tusi couple.

originalEn.event: Born in Tus, Khorasan, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Takes refuge with the Nizari Isma'ilis (Assassins), eventually being held in Alamut Castle.
Alamut Castle falls to the Mongols. Tusi enters the service of the Mongol ruler Hulagu Khan.
Construction begins on the Maragheh observatory under Tusi's direction.
Completes his major work on ethics, Akhlaq-i Nasiri (The Nasirean Ethics).
The Maragheh observatory team completes the highly influential astronomical tables, the Zij-i Ilkhani.
Dies in Kadhimiya, near Baghdad, and is buried in a shrine next to the seventh Twelver Shia Imam.

originalEn.quote_en: Is not this the great Babylon I have built by my mighty power as a royal residence and for the glory of my majesty?
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his immense pride in his architectural achievements and his belief that his power was the source of Babylon's glory.

originalEn.quote_en: A rebellious city is a lesson to its neighbors. Let its dust be a warning that loyalty to Babylon is the only path to peace.
originalEn.meaning_en: This captures his policy of using overwhelming force and destruction (as with Jerusalem) as a deterrent to other potential rebels.

originalEn.quote_en: To honor Marduk, my lord, I have clad the walls of his sacred precinct with shining gold and made them glisten like the sun. For a king's greatness is measured by his piety.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects the common practice of Mesopotamian kings to attribute their building projects to their devotion to the gods, linking royal power with divine favor.

originalEn.event: Birth of Nebuchadnezzar II.
Defeats Egyptians at the Battle of Carchemish; ascends to the throne of Babylon.
First siege of Jerusalem. Deports King Jehoiachin and other elites to Babylon.
Second siege of Jerusalem. Destroys the city and Solomon's Temple, beginning the Babylonian Captivity.
Conducts a 13-year siege of the city of Tyre.
Reigns as king, overseeing massive construction projects in Babylon, including the Ishtar Gate.
Dies in Babylon, succeeded by his son Amel-Marduk.

originalEn.quote_en: A kingdom can last with irreligion, but it cannot last with injustice.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes that justice and fair governance are more crucial for a state's stability than religious uniformity. It is a pragmatic principle of statecraft.

originalEn.quote_en: The ruler is a protector, and the people are the protected.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights the ruler's fundamental duty to care for and safeguard their subjects, establishing a social contract based on responsibility.

originalEn.quote_en: One of the duties of a king is to be acquainted with the religious law, with the traditions and commands of God.
originalEn.meaning_en: While justice is paramount, a ruler must also be grounded in moral and religious principles to govern wisely and maintain legitimacy.

originalEn.event: Born Abu Ali Hasan ibn Ali Tusi in Tus, Persia.
Appointed as vizier to the Seljuk Sultan Alp Arslan.
Secures the throne for Malik-Shah I after Alp Arslan's assassination, becoming the empire's most powerful figure.
Founds the Nizamiyyah schools across the empire, with the most famous one in Baghdad established in 1065.
Writes the Siyasatnama (Book of Government), a major work on statecraft.
Assassinated near Nahavand, Persia, believed to be by a member of the Order of Assassins.

originalEn.quote_en: A true ruler does not stop at expanding territory with armed force, but establishes a massive order where roads connect and laws reach.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means the governing power that completed a firm system of an empire where various peoples live together in harmony by reorganizing the postal system and unifying currency.

originalEn.quote_en: Scars carved by a sword heal, but truths and achievements carved on a rock do not collapse even in the wind and rain of thousands of years.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the wisdom of leaving multilingual records on a sheer cliff so that future generations will forever remember the history and majesty of the empire he achieved.

originalEn.quote_en: Just as a massive tower goes up when stones of various sizes interlock, an empire is finally completed when different cultures are respected.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows the embrace that led to the peace and prosperity of the empire by showing generous tolerance without suppressing the religion and customs of the conquered people.

originalEn.event: Birth of Darius.
Seizes the throne of the Achaemenid Empire after overthrowing the usurper Gaumata.
Suppresses widespread revolts across the empire.
Begins work on the Behistun Inscription to legitimize his rule.
Begins construction of the new ceremonial capital, Persepolis.
Launches an unsuccessful campaign against the Scythians in Europe.
The Ionian Revolt begins, marking the start of the Greco-Persian Wars.
The first Persian invasion of Greece ends in defeat at the Battle of Marathon.
Dies in Persia while preparing a second expedition against Greece.

originalEn.quote_en: To leave the practice of the salaf is to exit from the straight path of Islam.
originalEn.meaning_en: This means that true reform comes from emulating the pure practices of the first generations of Muslims, not from blindly imitating later traditions or Western ideas.

originalEn.quote_en: The Islamic government is a civilian government... it is not a theocracy, but its legislation is restricted by the fundamentals of Islam.
originalEn.meaning_en: This signifies his vision of a state governed by civilians, not clergy, but where laws must adhere to the core principles of the Quran and Sunnah.

originalEn.quote_en: The decline of the Muslims is due to their deviation from the true religion of Islam, which is a religion of knowledge, civilization, and strength.
originalEn.meaning_en: This shows his belief that the weakness of the Muslim world was not inherent to Islam, but a result of abandoning its true, intellectually vibrant principles.

originalEn.event: Born in al-Qalamoun, near Tripoli, in Ottoman Syria.
Moved to Cairo, Egypt, to collaborate with Muhammad Abduh.
Founded the influential journal Al-Manar (The Lighthouse).
Following Abduh's death, he became the leading voice of the Islamic Modernist movement.
Founded the 'Society for Preaching and Guidance' in Cairo.
Elected President of the Syrian National Congress.
The abolition of the Ottoman Caliphate deeply impacted his later political thought.
Died in Cairo, Egypt.

originalEn.quote_en: A nation is an army. If it is not, it is nothing.
originalEn.meaning_en: Reflects his military background and nationalist belief that a nation's strength and survival depend on a powerful, organized military force.

originalEn.quote_en: I cannot be a Shah of a people who are ignorant.
originalEn.meaning_en: Shows his strong conviction in the importance of education for modernization. He believed an educated populace was key to national progress.

originalEn.quote_en: My country is my only religion.
originalEn.meaning_en: Emphasizes his intense secular nationalism, prioritizing national identity over religious identity. He placed the unity and sovereignty of Iran above all else.

originalEn.event: Reza Khan is born in Alasht, Mazandaran Province, Persia.
Leads a coup d'état with the Persian Cossack Brigade, becoming Minister of War.
Is voted Shah by the Majlis (parliament), ending the Qajar dynasty and founding the Pahlavi dynasty.
Officially requests foreign nations to use the name 'Iran' instead of 'Persia' in formal correspondence.
Establishes the University of Tehran, a cornerstone of his educational reforms.
Issues the 'Kashf-e hijab' decree, controversially banning all veils in public.
Is forced to abdicate by the Anglo-Soviet invasion of Iran during World War II.
Dies in exile in Johannesburg, South Africa.

originalEn.quote_en: There are two principles, one of Light and one of Darkness, which were separate from each other at the beginning.
originalEn.meaning_en: This explains the core dualistic belief of Manichaeism: that the universe is an eternal battleground between a good, spiritual world of Light and an evil, material world of Darkness.

originalEn.quote_en: The religion which I have chosen is in ten things superior to the other, previous religions.
originalEn.meaning_en: Mani believed he was the final 'Seal of the Prophets,' completing the revelations of all previous prophets, including Zoroaster, Buddha, and Jesus. His religion was meant to be the final, universal message for all humanity.

originalEn.quote_en: The soul must be liberated from its imprisonment in matter and return to the Kingdom of Light.
originalEn.meaning_en: This summarizes the soteriological goal of Manichaeism. The human soul is a particle of divine light, and through an ascetic life and knowledge (gnosis), it can escape its bondage in the material world and return to its divine origin.

originalEn.event: Born in Mardinu, Babylonia, within the Parthian Empire.
Receives his first revelation from his spiritual twin, the Syzygos.
Receives his second revelation, commanding him to begin his public ministry.
Travels to the Kushan Empire in modern-day Afghanistan and India, encountering Buddhist thought.
Returns to Persia and is granted an audience with the newly crowned Sasanian Emperor, Shapur I, gaining his favor.
Preaches and spreads his religion throughout the Sasanian Empire and beyond under royal protection.
Accused of heresy by the Zoroastrian clergy, he is imprisoned and executed by order of Emperor Bahram I.

originalEn.quote_en: Henceforth, I will only distinguish my subjects, the Muslim in the mosque, the Christian in the church, the Jew in the synagogue.
originalEn.meaning_en: This statement signifies his commitment to equality and civil parity for all religious groups within the empire, foreshadowing a key principle of the Tanzimat reforms.

originalEn.quote_en: A custom is not a divine command.
originalEn.meaning_en: This quote reflects his pragmatic attitude toward outdated and inefficient traditions, justifying necessary reforms for the survival of the empire.

originalEn.quote_en: To build the new, the old foundation, however storied, must sometimes be razed to the ground.
originalEn.meaning_en: This captures the essence of his radical actions, like the destruction of the Janissaries, showing a belief that true progress requires a decisive break with the past.

originalEn.event: Born in Constantinople.
Ascends to the throne after the overthrow of his brother, Mustafa IV.
The 'Auspicious Incident' (Vaka-i Hayriye), where he abolishes the Janissary corps.
The Treaty of Adrianople concludes the Russo-Turkish War, granting autonomy to Serbia and Greece.
Establishes the first official Ottoman newspaper, Takvim-i Vekayi (Calendar of Events).
Dies of tuberculosis, shortly before his planned Edict of Gülhane (part of the Tanzimat) is proclaimed.

originalEn.quote_en: Either I will take Constantinople, or Constantinople will take me.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his unwavering determination and the high stakes he set for himself, viewing the conquest as a defining, all-or-nothing moment for his reign and legacy.

originalEn.quote_en: The world is a single state. It must have a single sovereign.
originalEn.meaning_en: This showcases his vast ambition, not just to conquer territory, but to establish a universal empire under his rule, a concept influenced by Roman and Mongol traditions.

originalEn.quote_en: To be a ruler, it is not enough to have a sword in your hand. You must also know how to use it.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights his belief that power requires not just military might but also wisdom, strategy, and the political skill to govern effectively and enact lasting change.

originalEn.event: Born in Edirne, the Ottoman capital at the time.
First ascends the throne at age 12 after his father's abdication, but is deposed in favor of his father due to court intrigue and external threats.
Ascends the throne for the second time upon the death of his father, Murad II.
Conquers Constantinople after a 53-day siege, effectively ending the Byzantine Empire and making it the new Ottoman capital, Istanbul.
Conquers the Empire of Trebizond, the last major Byzantine successor state.
Wages a long and costly war with the Republic of Venice for control of the Aegean and Adriatic seas.
Compiles the 'Kanunname,' a code of laws that centralized power and shaped the structure of the Ottoman state for centuries.
Dies suddenly during a new military campaign, with the cause of death remaining a subject of historical debate (poison or natural causes).

originalEn.quote_en: Peace at home, peace in the world.
originalEn.meaning_en: This is my foreign policy principle, stating that domestic stability and peace are the foundation for international peace. It means a nation must first be strong and stable internally to coexist peacefully with the outside world.

originalEn.quote_en: Our true mentor in life is science.
originalEn.meaning_en: This statement underscores my commitment to secularism, rationalism, and modernization. I believed that we must move away from superstition and outdated customs, and instead use science and reason as the guiding principles for national progress.

originalEn.quote_en: Teachers are the one and only people who save nations.
originalEn.meaning_en: I believed that education was the most critical element in shaping a nation's future. Teachers play the essential role of cultivating future generations who will understand and practice the ideals of the republic.

originalEn.event: Born in Salonica (now Thessaloniki, Greece), Ottoman Empire.
Commands Ottoman forces to victory at the Battle of Gallipoli, gaining national fame.
Initiates the Turkish War of Independence against occupying Allied Powers.
Establishes the Grand National Assembly of Turkey in Ankara.
Abolishes the Ottoman Sultanate.
Proclaims the Republic of Turkey and is elected as its first President.
Initiates the replacement of the Arabic-based Ottoman Turkish script with the Latin-based Turkish alphabet.
Grants full universal suffrage to women and is given the surname 'Atatürk' (Father of the Turks) by the parliament.
Dies in Dolmabahçe Palace, Istanbul, at the age of 57.

originalEn.quote_en: I was born in the same year as Napoleon, and I too have my ambitions.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his immense personal ambition and his view of himself as a major historical actor on the world stage, comparable to the greatest European leaders of his time.

originalEn.quote_en: The whole of the revenue is mine; and the whole of the expense is mine.
originalEn.meaning_en: This statement bluntly illustrates his policy of state monopoly over the entire Egyptian economy, which was the financial engine for his military and industrial reforms.

originalEn.quote_en: My country is no longer in Africa; we are now part of Europe.
originalEn.meaning_en: This expresses his core vision of transforming Egypt from a traditional Ottoman province into a modern, Westernized state capable of competing with European powers on its own terms.

originalEn.event: Born in Kavala, Ottoman Empire (now in Greece).
Arrives in Egypt as second-in-command of an Albanian contingent of the Ottoman army.
Appointed Wāli (governor) of Egypt by the Ottoman Sultan after popular demand.
Consolidates power by massacring the Mamluk leaders at the Citadel in Cairo.
Wages war against the Wahhabis in Arabia on behalf of the Sultan, extending his influence.
Conquers Sudan, seeking gold, slaves, and recruits for his army.
Intervenes in the Greek War of Independence, but his navy is destroyed at the Battle of Navarino.
First Egyptian-Ottoman War. His son Ibrahim Pasha conquers Syria and advances into Anatolia.
Second Egyptian-Ottoman War. European intervention forces him to withdraw from Syria but grants his family hereditary rule of Egypt.
Steps down from power due to failing health.
Dies in Alexandria.

originalEn.quote_en: I went to the West and saw Islam, but no Muslims; I got back to the East and saw Muslims, but no Islam.
originalEn.meaning_en: This quote criticizes the state of Muslim societies by observing that the ideal values of Islam—such as order, cleanliness, and honesty—were more visible in Western societies, while Muslims themselves were not living up to these principles.

originalEn.quote_en: To truly raise a nation, you must first raise its mothers.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes the critical importance of female education. Abduh believed that educated mothers would raise an enlightened next generation, which he saw as the fundamental basis for national progress.

originalEn.quote_en: The two books, that of God's revelation and that of God's creation, cannot contradict one another.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects the belief that religious revelation (the Qur'an) and the natural world (science and reason) both originate from God and therefore must be in harmony. It is an assertion that faith and reason are compatible.

originalEn.event: Born in a village in Lower Egypt.
Enrolled at Al-Azhar University in Cairo.
Met his influential mentor, Jamal al-Din al-Afghani.
Exiled from Egypt for his support of the 'Urabi Revolt.
Co-founded the pan-Islamic journal 'Al-Urwah al-Wuthqa' in Paris.
Returned to Egypt from exile.
Appointed Grand Mufti of Egypt, the nation's highest authority on Islamic law.
Died in Alexandria, Egypt.

originalEn.quote_en: Architecture begins with fulfilling a need, and ends as an art that expresses faith and authority.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his philosophy that the essence of architecture must transcend practical purposes to become a symbol of the era's spirit, faith, and power.

originalEn.quote_en: Under a perfect dome, there must be a space without pillars, where all souls can become one without obstruction.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes the importance of the vast, unified interior spaces he pursued. It shows his intention to create not just a physical structure, but a space for the spiritual unification of the community.

originalEn.quote_en: My work as an apprentice was the Şehzade Mosque in Istanbul, my work as a journeyman was the Süleymaniye Mosque, and my masterpiece as a master is the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne.
originalEn.meaning_en: Sinan himself evaluated his career in three stages. This demonstrates his craftsmanship and his spirit of striving for perfection through continuous learning and development.

originalEn.event: Born in the village of Ağırnas near Kayseri, Anatolia.
Recruited into the Ottoman Janissary corps through the Devşirme system.
Appointed as the Chief Imperial Architect by Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent.
Completed the Şehzade Mosque in Istanbul, which he considered his first major work.
Completed the Süleymaniye Mosque in Istanbul, a symbol of the Ottoman golden age.
Completed the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne, which he regarded as his masterpiece.
Died in Istanbul at an advanced age and was buried in a tomb of his own design near the Süleymaniye Mosque.

originalEn.quote_en: Let me fleece this flock, before they get away.
originalEn.meaning_en: A cynical but witty observation on seizing opportunities, characteristic of his roguish protagonist.

originalEn.quote_en: Poetry is a bride whose face is unveiled only to the one who has courted her with long vigils.
originalEn.meaning_en: True mastery of language and art requires immense dedication and effort.

originalEn.quote_en: The world is a stage, and its people are but players in a tale told by a clever tongue.
originalEn.meaning_en: Reflects the themes of his work, suggesting that life can be seen as a series of performances and stories, where wit and eloquence often determine the outcome.

originalEn.event: Born in Hamadan, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Leaves Hamadan and travels through the Buyid empire, seeking knowledge and patronage.
Arrives in Nishapur and famously debates the scholar Abu Bakr al-Khwarizmi, earning his title 'Badi' al-Zaman'.
Composes his most famous work, the Maqamat, inventing a new literary genre.
Dies in Herat (in modern-day Afghanistan) at the young age of 38.

originalEn.quote_en: All things are beautiful, either in themselves or in their results.
originalEn.meaning_en: This means finding divine beauty and purpose even in things that seem ugly or painful. Every event holds a positive outcome or lesson.

originalEn.quote_en: The universe is a great book, and we are its readers.
originalEn.meaning_en: This encourages the study of creation (science) as a way to understand the Creator. Observing nature is like reading God's artistry and wisdom.

originalEn.quote_en: He who sees the good in a thing, sees the good in everything.
originalEn.meaning_en: A positive perspective has the power to transform any experience. Faith provides a lens through which we can see God's goodness in all things.

originalEn.event: Born in Nurs, Bitlis Vilayet, Ottoman Empire.
Travels to Istanbul, proposes educational reforms to combine religious and modern sciences.
Exiled to Burdur, then the remote village of Barla, beginning a 25-year period of exile and imprisonment.
Writes the majority of the Risale-i Nur collection while in exile in Barla.
Faces his first major trial and imprisonment in Eskişehir, along with many of his students.
Dies in Urfa, Turkey. His grave was later moved to an unknown location by the military government.

originalEn.quote_en: A king must be a lion in battle and a fox in council.
originalEn.meaning_en: Leadership requires both overwhelming force and cunning strategy. One must be bold on the battlefield and wise in politics.

originalEn.quote_en: Destiny may place you in chains, but it is your will that forges the key.
originalEn.meaning_en: One's origins do not define their ultimate potential; personal drive is paramount.

originalEn.quote_en: A bridge built is a stronger defense than a wall.
originalEn.meaning_en: Connecting your empire through trade and communication builds more lasting strength than simply isolating it.

originalEn.event: Born a Kipchak Turk on the Pontic-Caspian Steppe.
Sold as a slave in Syria, eventually purchased by the Ayyubid sultan of Egypt.
Plays a key role in defeating the Seventh Crusade at the Battle of Al Mansurah.
Leads the vanguard of the Mamluk army to a decisive victory over the Mongols at the Battle of Ain Jalut.
Assassinates Sultan Qutuz and ascends to the throne as the Mamluk Sultan.
Captures the Crusader city of Antioch, a major blow to the Crusader states.
Captures the formidable Crusader castles of Krak des Chevaliers and Montfort.
Dies in Damascus, possibly from poison.

originalEn.quote_en: The children of Adam are limbs of one body, having been created of one essence.
originalEn.meaning_en: This expresses the spirit of humanism, that all of humanity is fundamentally connected as one, and the suffering of one is the suffering of all.

originalEn.quote_en: Have patience. All things are difficult before they become easy.
originalEn.meaning_en: This advises perseverance and resilience, reminding us that initial struggles are a natural part of any worthwhile endeavor.

originalEn.quote_en: A little and a little, collected, make a great deal.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights the power of consistent, small efforts over time, applicable to learning, saving, or any form of personal growth.

originalEn.event: Born in Shiraz, Persia.
Leaves Shiraz to study in Baghdad at the Nizamiyya University following the Mongol invasions.
Embarks on a thirty-year journey across the Middle East, North Africa, and Anatolia.
Completes his epic poem 'Bustan' (The Orchard).
Completes his most famous work, 'Gulistan' (The Rose Garden).
Dies in his hometown of Shiraz.

originalEn.quote_en: A single will can unite a thousand cities.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his achievement of unifying the fragmented Sumerian city-states into a single empire.

originalEn.quote_en: Power is not given, it is forged in the crucible of ambition and action.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights his rise from a commoner to an emperor, earning his station through his own efforts.

originalEn.quote_en: To rule an empire is to command the roads, the trade, and the language of its people.
originalEn.meaning_en: This points to his administrative reforms, including standardizing measures, building roads, and making Akkadian the lingua franca.
