originalEn.event: Rises to power, overthrows Ur-Zababa of Kish, and founds the city of Akkad.
Conducts a series of military campaigns, conquering Sumerian city-states like Uruk, Ur, and Lagash.
Establishes the Akkadian Empire, uniting Mesopotamia and creating the world's first known empire.
Dies after a 56-year reign, passing the empire to his son, Rimush.

originalEn.quote_en: A victory is not merely won on the battlefield; it is carved into stone to teach generations the meaning of strength.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects Shapur I's practice of documenting his triumphs in the rock reliefs at Naqsh-e Rostam. It signifies that a true victory transcends military success; it's about creating a lasting legacy that inspires future generations and immortalizes the empire's greatness.

originalEn.quote_en: An empire is built not only with soldiers, but with the skills of all its people, regardless of their origin or faith.
originalEn.meaning_en: This wisdom illustrates Shapur I's pragmatic and tolerant approach to governance. By utilizing the engineering skills of Roman prisoners and embracing different faiths like Manichaeism, he understood that an empire's strength lies in its ability to integrate diversity and harness the talents of all.

originalEn.quote_en: Let them build walls of stone; I shall build a legacy of cities and knowledge that will outlast them.
originalEn.meaning_en: This quote highlights Shapur's dedication to city-building, including centers of learning like Gundeshapur. It suggests his belief that cultural and intellectual development creates a more enduring power and legacy than mere physical fortifications.

originalEn.event: Born to Ardashir I, the founder of the Sasanian Empire.
Ascends to the throne as the second Sasanian King of Kings.
Defeats the Roman army under Emperor Gordian III at the Battle of Misiche.
Conducts his second campaign against Rome, sacking the major city of Antioch.
Achieves his most famous victory at the Battle of Edessa, defeating and capturing the Roman Emperor Valerian.
Founds the city of Bishapur, using captured Roman soldiers and engineers for its construction.
Dies, leaving behind a greatly expanded and strengthened empire.

originalEn.quote_en: The only way to save a declining empire lies in the courage to discard the old and embrace the new.
originalEn.meaning_en: Selim III believed that the empire's survival depended not on clinging to tradition, but on the ability to reform boldly in response to the changing times.

originalEn.quote_en: The strongest ramparts are not made of stone, but of well-trained and loyal soldiers.
originalEn.meaning_en: He realized the empire could no longer be defended by the old, corrupt Janissaries, and emphasized the importance of a modern, effective military force.

originalEn.quote_en: The path of reform is always met with the fiercest resistance.
originalEn.meaning_en: Through his own experience, he deeply understood that powerful opposition from vested interests was an inevitable fate when pursuing meaningful change.

originalEn.event: Born in Constantinople, son of Sultan Mustafa III.
Ascends to the Ottoman throne during the Austro-Turkish and Russo-Turkish wars.
Signs the Treaty of Jassy, ending the war with Russia and allowing him to focus on domestic reforms.
Formally establishes the 'Nizam-ı Cedid' (New Order) to modernize the state, starting with a new army.
Faces the French invasion of Ottoman Egypt and Syria, led by Napoleon Bonaparte.
Deposed by a Janissary revolt led by Kabakçı Mustafa, and his reforms are abolished.
Assassinated in the Topkapı Palace on the orders of his successor, Mustafa IV, during an attempt to restore him to the throne.

originalEn.quote_en: Only one who boldly shakes the roots of the system without settling for corrupt vested interests can revive the vitality of a nation.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means the will for reform that made a declining nation powerful again by checking the powerful forces and introducing a new military system and administrative framework.

originalEn.quote_en: A splendid city must not simply be a display of power, but must become a creative heart where talents and merchants from all over the world gather.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the discerning eye that achieved the empire's economic and cultural golden age by moving the capital and nurturing it as a center for beautiful architecture and commerce.

originalEn.quote_en: Encouraging art and philosophy internally and unfolding practical diplomacy externally is the unshakeable barrier of an empire.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows balanced governance that opened a golden age of culture by patronizing artists and defeated enemies by forming practical alliances with external powers.

originalEn.event: Born in Herat, modern-day Afghanistan.
Ascends the Safavid throne at the age of 16 amidst a military coup against his father.
Signs a harsh peace treaty with the Ottoman Empire, ceding large territories to buy time for internal reforms.
Moves the capital from Qazvin to Isfahan and defeats the Uzbeks near Herat, securing the eastern frontier.
Wages a successful war against the Ottoman Empire, reclaiming territories including Baghdad, Azerbaijan, and Georgia.
With the help of the English navy, captures the strategic island of Hormuz from the Portuguese.
Recaptures Baghdad from the Ottomans.
Dies in Mazandaran, leaving a powerful but unstable legacy due to the lack of a capable heir.

originalEn.quote_en: The wisdom of Nabu, the art of the scribe in its entirety, I have written, collated, and checked, and I have placed it in my library for the inspection of my people.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects Ashurbanipal's dedication to knowledge collection and preservation, and that his library was an asset for the empire, not just a personal collection.

originalEn.quote_en: I am Ashurbanipal, the great king, the mighty king, king of the universe, king of Assyria, king of the four quarters of the world.
originalEn.meaning_en: This is his standard royal titulary, asserting his vast power and divine right to rule, granted by the gods.

originalEn.quote_en: I can read the intricate tablets from before the flood; I can fell a lion with my bow as I can solve a complex omen.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights Ashurbanipal's dual identity as both a scholar and a warrior, taking pride in his intellectual and physical prowess.

originalEn.event: Born in Nineveh, a younger son of King Esarhaddon.
Ascends to the throne of Assyria after the death of his father.
Conducts major campaigns in Egypt, culminating in the sack of Thebes.
A devastating civil war begins with his older brother, Shamash-shum-ukin, ruler of Babylon.
Defeats his brother and sacks the city of Babylon, ending the civil war.
Sacks the Elamite capital of Susa, effectively destroying the rival kingdom of Elam.
Dies, leaving the Assyrian Empire at its peak but severely overstretched and internally weakened.

originalEn.quote_en: Stand for the teacher and grant him reverence, for the teacher was almost a messenger.
originalEn.meaning_en: Emphasizes the sacred and vital role of educators in society, placing them on a near-prophetic level.

originalEn.quote_en: The small nations are the dust of history.
originalEn.meaning_en: A stark reminder of the vulnerability of nations without power and unity, urging for national strength.

originalEn.quote_en: And for the homelands in the blood of every free man, is a hand that protects it and a tongue that speaks for it.
originalEn.meaning_en: True patriotism involves both action (protection) and voice (advocacy) from its free citizens.

originalEn.event: Born in Cairo, Egypt, to a wealthy family of mixed Turkish, Arab, Circassian, Kurdish, and Greek roots.
Graduated from the School of Law and was sent by Khedive Tewfik to study in France for three years.
Returned to Egypt and was appointed to a prominent position in the court of Khedive Abbas II.
Forced into exile by the British authorities due to his nationalist poetry and support for Egyptian independence. He chose to live in Andalusia, Spain.
Returned to Egypt from exile and was hailed as a national hero.
Crowned 'Amir al-Shu'ara' (The Prince of Poets) by his peers in a grand ceremony, acknowledging his status as the foremost poet in the Arab world.
Died in Cairo at the age of 64.

originalEn.quote_en: The science of history is the mother of all sciences.
originalEn.meaning_en: This signifies that an understanding of the past is the essential foundation for grasping the present and planning for the future.

originalEn.quote_en: Justice is the foundation of the state.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects the belief that no state can prosper or endure without a fair and reliable legal system.

originalEn.quote_en: To change with the times is a necessity, but to abandon one's roots is to lose oneself.
originalEn.meaning_en: This summarizes his philosophy of reform: progress is vital, but it should not come at the cost of one's cultural and historical identity.

originalEn.event: Born in Lofça (now Lovech, Bulgaria).
Arrived in Constantinople to pursue higher education.
Appointed official historian of the Ottoman Empire.
Appointed head of the Mecelle Commission to codify Ottoman civil law.
Served his first of several terms as Minister of Justice.
Died in Constantinople.

originalEn.quote_en: When treating a patient, consider not only the disease, but also the patient's mind and environment.
originalEn.meaning_en: Rhazes emphasized the importance of holistic care, considering a patient's psychological state and living conditions, not just the physical symptoms of a disease.

originalEn.quote_en: If you are in doubt, do not be afraid to try something new and to question old authorities.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects Rhazes' scientific attitude, which valued experimentation and empirical evidence. He believed knowledge advances through rational doubt and verification, not blind faith.

originalEn.quote_en: A true physician is one who gives hope to the patient and does their utmost to alleviate their suffering.
originalEn.meaning_en: Rhazes considered ethical responsibility and empathy to be as important as a physician's technical skills, a theme prominent in his work 'The Ethics of a Physician'.

originalEn.event: Born in Ray, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Appointed chief physician of the hospital in Ray.
Moved to Baghdad and was appointed director of the main hospital, which he located using an experiment involving meat decomposition.
Wrote 'A Treatise on Smallpox and Measles' (Kitab al-Judari wa al-Hasbah), the first text to clinically differentiate the two diseases.
Died in his hometown of Ray. His students later compiled his notes into the 'Kitab al-Hawi' (The Comprehensive Book).

originalEn.quote_en: The most miserable of men is he who is deprived of the power of learning.
originalEn.meaning_en: Knowledge is a fundamental human need, and its absence leads to true poverty.

originalEn.quote_en: I saw Aristotle in a dream, and he told me, 'The good is that which is good in the mind.'
originalEn.meaning_en: The belief that true value and goodness are determined by reason and intellect, which encouraged him to seek out rationalist Greek philosophy.

originalEn.quote_en: Let the people be free in their beliefs.
originalEn.meaning_en: This is somewhat ironic given the later 'Mihna', but it reflects his earlier, more tolerant stance and the general spirit of intellectual inquiry he fostered.

originalEn.event: Born in Baghdad, son of the fifth Abbasid Caliph, Harun al-Rashid.
Harun al-Rashid dies. Al-Ma'mun's half-brother, al-Amin, becomes caliph in Baghdad, while al-Ma'mun governs the eastern province of Khurasan.
The Fourth Fitna, a civil war between al-Amin and al-Ma'mun, culminates in the Siege of Baghdad and al-Ma'mun's victory.
Founds the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) in Baghdad, a major center for translation and research.
Sponsors an expedition of astronomers to measure the Earth's circumference, achieving a remarkably accurate result for the time.
Initiates the 'Mihna' (inquisition) to enforce Mu'tazilite doctrine. Dies during a military campaign against the Byzantine Empire.

originalEn.quote_en: Only one who escapes the narrow sky inside a well and walks the world directly on two feet can gain true and three-dimensional knowledge.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means the empirical attitude of recording vivid history and geography by directly exploring vast continents and oceans without relying solely on literature.

originalEn.quote_en: The breath of the times is contained in the lives and customs of all people, and history must remember the stories of the people more than the names of kings.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the insight that achieved broad academic accomplishments by deeply observing not only political events but also the daily lives and environments of diverse cultures.

originalEn.quote_en: True learning finally begins with an open mind that takes off the glasses of prejudice and respects the culture of an unfamiliar other.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows an attitude that elevated academic value by describing various civilizations with an objective and inclusive perspective, unbound by religion or race.

originalEn.event: Born in Baghdad, Abbasid Caliphate, a major center of learning.
Begins his extensive travels, which would last for over two decades and cover vast regions of Asia and Africa.
Travels through Persia and into the Indus Valley in India, documenting local customs and history.
Completes the first version of his major work, 'Muruj al-Dhahab' (The Meadows of Gold).
Settles in Fustat (Old Cairo), Egypt, where he spends his later years writing and revising his works.
Dies in Fustat, Egypt, leaving behind a monumental but largely lost body of work.

originalEn.quote_en: The celestial bodies move in accordance with a divine order; precise observation is the first step to understanding it.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes the importance of empirical evidence and meticulous data collection over theory or belief. True knowledge comes from direct observation.

originalEn.quote_en: Even the greatest authorities of the past are not infallible. Truth is found not in blind acceptance, but in rigorous verification.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects the belief that scientific inquiry requires a willingness to question established knowledge, constantly testing and refining it.

originalEn.quote_en: Mathematics is the language with which God has written the universe. Through it, we can read the heavens.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights the fundamental role of mathematics in understanding and describing the physical phenomena of the universe. The order of the cosmos can be deciphered through mathematical principles.

originalEn.event: Born in Harran, Upper Mesopotamia (modern-day Turkey).
Moved to Raqqa, Syria, and began his major astronomical observations.
Completed his magnum opus, the Kitāb az-Zīj aṣ-Ṣābi’ (The Sabian Astronomical Tables).
His work was first translated into Latin by Plato of Tivoli as 'De Motu Stellarum' (On the Motion of the Stars).
Died near Samarra, Iraq, while returning from a trip to Baghdad.

originalEn.quote_en: A book is a master who is silent when you need it, and speaks when you desire it.
originalEn.meaning_en: This means that a book is the ultimate source of knowledge, providing wisdom at the reader's command without being intrusive, and is always accessible.

originalEn.quote_en: Eloquence is not merely to speak much, but to speak in season.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes that true eloquence lies not in the quantity of words, but in the ability to say the right thing at the right time.

originalEn.quote_en: He who does not understand a joke will not be reached by a serious discourse.
originalEn.meaning_en: This suggests that the ability to understand humor and wit is part of the same intellect required to grasp deeper, more serious concepts.

originalEn.event: Born in Basra (modern-day Iraq).
Moves to Baghdad and begins to gain recognition in the intellectual circles of the Abbasid Caliphate.
Composes his major works, including the 'Book of Animals' (Kitab al-Hayawan) and the 'Book of Eloquence and Exposition' (Kitab al-Bayan wa-al-Tabyin).
Is briefly appointed to an office in the Caliph's court by al-Mutawakkil, but resigns after only three days.
Dies in his hometown of Basra at an advanced age.

originalEn.quote_en: We ought not to be ashamed of appreciating the truth and of acquiring it from wherever it comes, even if it comes from races distant and nations different from us.
originalEn.meaning_en: This quote showcases Al-Kindi's universalist and open-minded approach to knowledge and truth. He believed that the value of truth is not determined by its origin or cultural background.

originalEn.quote_en: The highest of the human arts is the art of the philosopher.
originalEn.meaning_en: Al-Kindi viewed philosophy as the pinnacle of all knowledge. He believed it leads humans to their highest intellectual and moral perfection by investigating the nature of reality and the purpose of human existence.

originalEn.quote_en: Nothing is more precious to the seeker of truth than the truth itself, nor is the truth ever debased or humbled by anyone who speaks it or brings it.
originalEn.meaning_en: This statement emphasizes the intrinsic value and objectivity of truth itself. It reflects his belief that truth is not affected by the person who delivers it or the opinions people may have about it.

originalEn.event: Born in Kufa, Abbasid Caliphate, to an aristocratic family of the Kindah tribe.
Flourished in Baghdad under Caliph al-Ma'mun, working at the House of Wisdom to translate and study Greek philosophical and scientific texts.
Continued his scholarly work under the patronage of Caliph al-Mu'tasim.
Fell out of favor during the reign of Caliph al-Mutawakkil. He faced persecution, and his personal library was temporarily confiscated.
His library and favor were restored, allowing him to resume his work.
Died in Baghdad, leaving a legacy of over 260 written works.

originalEn.quote_en: I am only transmitting what has been transmitted to me. The responsibility for its truthfulness lies entirely with its source.
originalEn.meaning_en: This quote reveals al-Tabari's methodology as a historian. He saw his role not as a judge of facts, but as a faithful transmitter of reports from various sources.

originalEn.quote_en: He who seeks knowledge is never satisfied.
originalEn.meaning_en: This summarizes al-Tabari's scholarly attitude, having dedicated his entire life to the pursuit of knowledge. It implies that true scholarship comes from endless inquiry and humility.

originalEn.quote_en: Every interpretation is but an attempt to approach the divine intent, not the final truth itself.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his approach in his 'Tafsir' (Quranic commentary). He presented various interpretations side-by-side, acknowledging the limits of human interpretation and opening space for the reader's own reflection.

originalEn.event: Born in Amol, Tabaristan (modern-day Mazandaran Province, Iran).
Left home at age 12 to study in Rayy, then traveled to Baghdad.
Traveled extensively through Syria, Palestine, and Egypt to collect hadith and historical reports.
Settled permanently in Baghdad, where he spent the rest of his life teaching and writing.
Completed his monumental history, 'Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk' (History of the Prophets and Kings).
Faced persecution from the Hanbali school in Baghdad, leading to a form of house arrest.
Died in Baghdad at the age of approximately 83-84.

originalEn.quote_en: I forgive you, and set you free.
originalEn.meaning_en: Said to the captured Byzantine Emperor Romanos IV, this shows remarkable magnanimity, breaking from the era's norms. It was not just mercy but a shrewd political move to psychologically dominate his foe and gain a political advantage.

originalEn.quote_en: Tonight I shall be in this camp, either as a ruler or as a martyr.
originalEn.meaning_en: Reportedly said in a speech to his soldiers before the Battle of Manzikert. It shows his resolve to fight to the death, a powerful display of leadership that boosted his troops' morale to its peak.

originalEn.quote_en: Beware... never to despise an enemy, however weak and insignificant he may be.
originalEn.meaning_en: His purported last words, spoken after being fatally wounded by a prisoner he underestimated. It's a tragic lesson from a great conqueror whose life of victory was ended by a single moment of carelessness.

originalEn.event: Born as Muhammad bin Dawud Chaghri.
Ascends to the throne of the Seljuk Empire after the death of his uncle, Tughril.
Captures Ani, the capital of Armenia, expanding Seljuk influence into the Caucasus.
Achieves a decisive victory over the Byzantine Empire at the Battle of Manzikert and captures Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes.
Mortally wounded by a prisoner during a campaign in Transoxiana and dies shortly after, succeeded by his son Malik-Shah I.

originalEn.quote_en: In a dream, I saw the Prophet Muhammad. Instead of asking for 'intercession' (şefaat), I misspoke and asked for 'travel' (seyahat). Thus my life's journey began.
originalEn.meaning_en: This famous anecdote illustrates how a fateful slip of the tongue initiated his lifelong, epic journey, symbolizing how deeply his identity was intertwined with travel.

originalEn.quote_en: A man who does not travel does not know the value of men.
originalEn.meaning_en: This summarizes his belief that firsthand experience with different cultures and people is essential to breaking down prejudice and fostering a deeper understanding and appreciation of humanity.

originalEn.quote_en: I have described everything I have seen, heard, and experienced, from the ant to the elephant, so that future generations may know.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his sense of historical mission. He believed his observations were more than personal memoirs; they were a vital repository of knowledge for future generations.

originalEn.event: Born in Constantinople (modern-day Istanbul) to a wealthy family with connections to the Ottoman court.
Reportedly has a dream of the Prophet Muhammad, which he interprets as a divine sign to embark on a life of travel.
Begins his first major journey outside of Constantinople, marking the start of four decades of extensive travel.
Travels extensively through the Ottoman Empire's territories in Anatolia, Rumelia, the Levant, Egypt, and neighboring lands including Persia, Austria, and Russia.
Dies, though the exact date and place are unknown. He is believed to have died in Constantinople or Cairo, having completed his 10-volume Seyahatname.

originalEn.quote_en: A book in the hand of one is a single lamp; a printed book in the hands of a thousand is a sun that illuminates the entire nation.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes the power of printing to scale knowledge from the few to the many, enlightening society as a whole.

originalEn.quote_en: To stand still while the world moves forward is to fall behind. We must adopt the useful tools of others to strengthen our own foundations.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his belief that embracing external innovations and technologies was essential for the empire's progress.

originalEn.quote_en: The purpose of this press is not to replace the holy script, but to multiply the useful sciences and histories, that our people may learn from the past and build a wiser future.
originalEn.meaning_en: He sought to reassure opponents by clarifying that his venture was not a threat to religion, but a complementary tool for the empire's intellectual growth.

originalEn.event: Born in Kolozsvár (now Cluj-Napoca, Romania).
Captured during a conflict and brought to the Ottoman Empire, where he converted to Islam.
Enters the Müteferrika corps, serving as a diplomat and envoy for the Sultan.
Submits his memorandum, 'Vesiletü't-Tıbâa' (The Usefulness of Printing), to the Grand Vizier.
Receives a fatwa and an imperial edict from Sultan Ahmed III, authorizing the printing of non-religious works.
Publishes the first book from his press, the 'Vankulu Lügati', a two-volume Arabic-Turkish dictionary.
Publishes Katip Çelebi's 'Cihannüma', a major work of geography, including new maps he engraved himself.
Dies in Constantinople (Istanbul). His press had printed 17 different titles by this time.

originalEn.quote_en: A commander must be the first in advance and the last in retreat.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes a fundamental principle of military leadership: a leader must always lead by example and take ultimate responsibility in the most difficult situations.

originalEn.quote_en: Victory on the battlefield is meaningless without order and justice in the lands you govern.
originalEn.meaning_en: This asserts that the ability to stabilize and justly rule conquered territory is as important as military conquest itself, a wisdom derived from his experience governing Syria.

originalEn.quote_en: The ambitions of one empire are always checked by the ambitions of others.
originalEn.meaning_en: A lesson learned from the European intervention that halted Egypt's expansion, reflecting the harsh reality of the balance of power and competition in international relations.

originalEn.event: Born in Kavala, Ottoman Rumelia (modern-day Greece).
Leads the Egyptian army in the Ottoman–Saudi War, defeating the First Saudi State.
Commands Egyptian forces in the Greek War of Independence, conquering the Morea.
His fleet is destroyed by a combined European force at the Battle of Navarino.
Leads the first Egyptian-Ottoman War, conquering Syria and advancing into Anatolia.
Decisively defeats the Ottoman army at the Battle of Konya.
Forced to withdraw from Syria by European powers under the Convention of London.
Becomes regent for his ailing father, but dies of tuberculosis after only a few months.

originalEn.quote_en: My heart has become capable of every form: it is a pasture for gazelles and a convent for Christian monks, and a temple for idols and the pilgrim's Ka'ba, and the tables of the Torah and the book of the Qur'an.
originalEn.meaning_en: This expresses a universal spiritual receptivity, embracing the divine truth found within all religious forms and symbols. The truly spiritual heart transcends divisive doctrines and sees the divine in all.

originalEn.quote_en: He who knows Him, there is none other than He. The knower is the one known Himself, and the one known is the knower Himself.
originalEn.meaning_en: This explains the core of the 'Unity of Being.' In the ultimate state of knowledge, the distinction between the knowing subject (the human) and the known object (God) dissolves, leaving only one unified Reality.

originalEn.quote_en: The universe could not contain Him, but He said, 'I am contained in the heart of My believing servant.'
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights God's immanence. While God is greater than the cosmos, He can reside within the purified heart of a believer, suggesting the human heart's potential to be a sacred space for the Divine Presence.

originalEn.event: Born in Murcia, al-Andalus (modern-day Spain).
His family moves to Seville, a major center of culture and learning.
Undergoes a profound spiritual conversion and begins his journey on the Sufi path.
Leaves the Iberian Peninsula permanently, beginning his extensive travels.
Arrives in Mecca, where he has a series of visions and begins writing his magnum opus, 'The Meccan Illuminations' (Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya).
Settles permanently in Damascus, which becomes his base for teaching and writing.
Completes 'The Bezels of Wisdom' (Fusus al-Hikam), a concise and highly influential work on his mystical philosophy.
Dies in Damascus and is buried at the foot of Mount Qasioun.

originalEn.quote_en: My name is Shah Ismail. I am God's mystery. I am the leader of all these ghazis (warriors).
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his dual identity as a political/military leader (Shah) and a divine, spiritual figure to his Qizilbash followers.

originalEn.quote_en: The world is a temporary inn, and man is a traveler.
originalEn.meaning_en: A common theme in the Sufi mystical tradition he emerged from, emphasizing the transient nature of worldly life in favor of spiritual truth.

originalEn.quote_en: Come, you who have turned your face from the Kaaba, come to me. I am the true Kaaba.
originalEn.meaning_en: A bold declaration of his spiritual authority, positioning himself as the center of religious devotion for his followers, superseding even traditional holy sites.

originalEn.event: Born in Ardabil.
Goes into hiding in Lahijan after his father and older brother are killed.
Conquers Tabriz and is enthroned as Shah of Azerbaijan at age 14.
Declared Shah of Iran. Makes Twelver Shi'ism the state religion.
Defeats the Uzbeks at the Battle of Merv, securing his eastern borders.
Suffers a decisive defeat against the Ottoman Empire at the Battle of Chaldiran.
Dies at the age of 36.

originalEn.quote_en: The life of a nation depends solely on the unity of its people.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes that a nation's strength and survival against external threats come not just from military might, but from internal cohesion and solidarity.

originalEn.quote_en: There is no incompatibility between religion and science.
originalEn.meaning_en: He argued that true Islam encourages rational inquiry and the pursuit of knowledge, and that Muslims must embrace modern science to regain their strength.

originalEn.quote_en: To cry out in the face of a tyrant is the greatest of holy wars (jihad).
originalEn.meaning_en: He framed political dissent and resistance against unjust rulers, both foreign and domestic, as a religious duty.

originalEn.event: Born in Asadabad, with his exact birthplace (Iran or Afghanistan) being a subject of historical debate.
Becomes an advisor to the ruler of Afghanistan, marking his early involvement in state politics.
Resides in Egypt, where he influences a generation of intellectuals, including Muhammad Abduh, before being exiled due to British pressure.
Co-founds the anti-colonial journal 'Al-Urwah al-Wuthqa' (The Firmest Bond) in Paris with Muhammad Abduh.
Active in Persia (Iran), his agitation against the Shah's tobacco concession to Britain leads to his expulsion and helps spark the Tobacco Protest.
Invited to Istanbul by Sultan Abdul Hamid II, where he remains under the Sultan's patronage and surveillance.
Dies of cancer in Istanbul. His remains were later moved to Afghanistan in 1944.

originalEn.quote_en: To realize the hidden logic of the world, what is needed is not blind belief but restless experiment and cool-headed observation.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means the academic attitude that laid the foundation of modern chemistry by elevating a field that had lingered in mysticism into the realm of systematic experimental science.

originalEn.quote_en: The desire to obtain gold from ordinary stones later becomes the key of wisdom that cures humanity's diseases and enriches life.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the fact that although the original purpose was not achieved, in the process, numerous substances and tools were invented, making a profound contribution to scientific development.

originalEn.quote_en: Knowledge gains eternal life when it is systematically recorded and passed on to the next generation, rather than when it stays in one person's head.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows the dedication of leaving his experimental processes and results in vast writings, inspiring countless scholars and continuing the pulse of learning.

originalEn.event: Born in Tus, Khorasan (modern-day Iran).
Active in Baghdad under the patronage of the Barmakid family, viziers to Caliph Harun al-Rashid.
The Barmakid family falls from power. Jabir is reportedly placed under house arrest in Kufa.
Dies in Kufa (in modern-day Iraq).
His works, under the Latinized name 'Geber,' are translated and begin to heavily influence European alchemy and science.

originalEn.quote_en: To be modern is to be international in civilization and national in culture.
originalEn.meaning_en: This illustrates his formula for modernization: Turkey should adopt Western science and technology ('civilization') while preserving its unique Turkish language, arts, and morals ('culture').

originalEn.quote_en: The nation is not a racial or ethnic or geographic or political or volitional group, but one composed of individuals who share a common language, religion, morality, and aesthetics.
originalEn.meaning_en: This defines his concept of a nation based on shared culture, not race. It was a significant departure from the ethnic nationalism prevalent in Europe at the time.

originalEn.quote_en: An individual cannot exist without a nation.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes his collectivist view where individual identity and purpose are derived from the national community. For him, the nation was essential for giving meaning to an individual's life.

originalEn.event: Born in Diyarbakır, Ottoman Empire.
Moves to Istanbul to study and becomes involved with the revolutionary Young Turk movement.
Becomes a leading member of the Committee of Union and Progress (CUP) in Salonica, the movement's intellectual hub.
Begins publishing his influential articles on sociology and Turkism, shaping the movement's ideology.
Arrested by British occupation forces and exiled to Malta along with other Ottoman nationalist leaders.
Released from exile and joins the Turkish nationalist movement in Ankara during the War of Independence.
Publishes his seminal work, 'The Principles of Turkism' (Türkçülüğün Esasları), codifying his ideology.
Dies in Istanbul, shortly after the founding of the Republic of Turkey, which was heavily influenced by his ideas.

originalEn.quote_en: I am not a king. I am merely the Vakil, the regent of the people.
originalEn.meaning_en: This showcases the core of his ruling philosophy: that his power existed not to dominate, but to serve and protect the populace.

originalEn.quote_en: A ruler's true wealth is not in his treasury, but in the prosperity of his subjects.
originalEn.meaning_en: This belief was the foundation of his economic policies, where he prioritized encouraging trade and public welfare over harsh taxation.

originalEn.quote_en: Justice is the foundation of a stable throne; without it, even the strongest walls will crumble.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes the importance he placed on establishing a fair and predictable rule of law to end a chaotic era and bring peace.

originalEn.event: Born in the village of Pari, near modern-day Malayer, to the Zand tribe.
Nader Shah is assassinated, plunging Iran into civil war. Karim Khan returns to his homeland and begins to consolidate power.
Becomes a major contender for power in western Iran after forming a tripartite alliance.
Establishes Shiraz as his capital after defeating all major rivals and controlling most of Iran. He adopts the title Vakil ol-Ro'aya (Regent of the People).
His reign is marked by peace and prosperity. He commissions major construction projects in Shiraz, including the Arg of Karim Khan, Vakil Bazaar, and Vakil Mosque.
Dies of natural causes in Shiraz. His death triggers succession struggles that lead to the downfall of the Zand dynasty.

originalEn.quote_en: Judging the world with a narrow perspective is covering one's own eyes, and true wisdom comes from broad learning and acceptance.
originalEn.meaning_en: It means the open attitude that tried to introduce new ideas and science to a closed society by delving into knowledge regardless of its origin.

originalEn.quote_en: The guide in a chaotic era is not flashy eloquence, but a single book that accurately records the world based on facts.
originalEn.meaning_en: It refers to the dedication of attempting to compile the knowledge of the times and pass on accurate information to future generations through vast bibliographic efforts and encyclopedic writings.

originalEn.quote_en: Ignorance gives birth to arrogance and prejudice, and deep learning builds a sturdy bridge of tolerance and understanding.
originalEn.meaning_en: It shows the insight that presented rational solutions by emphasizing objective and empirical academic inquiry to overcome social division and conflict.

originalEn.event: Born in Constantinople (modern-day Istanbul).
Appointed as a clerk in the imperial army's Anatolian financial bureaucracy.
Participated in Ottoman campaigns against the Safavids in Persia, traveling to Baghdad and Hamadan.
Inherited a large fortune, which allowed him to leave his bureaucratic post and pursue a life of scholarship.
Began compiling his monumental bibliographic work, 'Kashf al-Zunun' (The Removal of Doubt from the Names of Books and Arts).
Began writing his major geographical work, 'Jihannuma' (Mirror of the World).
Died in Constantinople, leaving several of his major works to be completed by others.

originalEn.quote_en: The will of a king moves mountains and parts seas. Even nature must bow to a great monarch.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects a belief in the absolute power and will of a monarch. It echoes his ambition in bridging the Hellespont and his hubristic character, as shown in the story of whipping the sea.

originalEn.quote_en: Vengeance is a sacred duty passed from father to son. The honor of the empire must be upheld, no matter the cost.
originalEn.meaning_en: This explains his motivation for invading Greece, continuing the mission of his father, Darius. It highlights not just personal revenge, but the responsibility of a ruler to maintain the empire's prestige.

originalEn.quote_en: Defeat in war is not the end. True rule is written in brick and stone to last a thousand years.
originalEn.meaning_en: This shows the shift in his approach to rule after the failure in Greece, moving from military expansion to grand construction. He sought to secure his legacy and the empire's permanence through physical monuments.

originalEn.event: Born to King Darius I and Atossa, a daughter of Cyrus the Great.
Ascends to the throne of the Achaemenid Empire after the death of his father, Darius I.
Crushes a major revolt in Egypt, asserting his authority.
Violently suppresses a revolt in Babylon, confiscating the sacred statue of Marduk.
Launches a massive invasion of Greece. Wins the Battle of Thermopylae, sacks Athens, but suffers a decisive naval defeat at the Battle of Salamis.
The Persian army left in Greece under his general Mardonius is decisively defeated at the Battle of Plataea, ending the invasion.
Focuses on ambitious construction projects, notably at Persepolis and Susa, completing many buildings his father had started.
Assassinated in a palace conspiracy led by Artabanus, the commander of his royal bodyguard.

originalEn.quote_en: The study of geometry is a way to purify the soul and prepare it for the ascent to the higher realms of knowledge.
originalEn.meaning_en: Reflects a Neoplatonic idea that mathematical pursuit is linked to spiritual and philosophical enlightenment, going beyond mere calculation.

originalEn.quote_en: I have extracted two other numbers (amicable numbers) by an easy and short method, and for these two numbers the same property holds.
originalEn.meaning_en: Highlights his practical, problem-solving approach to mathematics and his success in extending ancient knowledge.

originalEn.quote_en: The science of weights is a noble science, for it demonstrates with precision things that are not apparent to the senses.
originalEn.meaning_en: Emphasizes his belief in the power of mechanics and mathematics to reveal hidden truths about the physical world, a foundational principle of physics.

originalEn.event: Born in Harran, Upper Mesopotamia (modern-day Turkey).
Encounters Muhammad ibn Musa ibn Shakir, who invites him to Baghdad to work as a scholar.
Joins the circle of scholars in Baghdad, working under the patronage of the Banu Musa and later the Caliph al-Mu'tadid.
Produces major translations of Greek works (Euclid's Elements, Ptolemy's Almagest) and original treatises on mathematics, astronomy, and mechanics.
Dies in Baghdad, leaving a legacy carried on by his son, Sinan, and grandson, Ibrahim.

originalEn.quote_en: You can kill me as soon as you like, but you cannot stop the emancipation of women.
originalEn.meaning_en: Reportedly her last words, this statement encapsulates her unwavering belief that the movement for women's freedom was an unstoppable historical force, even if her own life was forfeit.

originalEn.quote_en: The fetters of the men I can dash asunder, The banners of the women I can hoist on the castles.
originalEn.meaning_en: This poetic verse expresses her revolutionary goal to break the chains of patriarchal oppression and elevate the status and power of women.

originalEn.quote_en: I am the blast of the trumpet, I am the call of the bugle!
originalEn.meaning_en: Here, she identifies herself not as a mere individual but as a powerful, divine signal—a herald announcing the arrival of a new spiritual age and a new social order.

originalEn.event: Born Fátimih Baraghání in Qazvin, Persia.
Becomes one of the first followers of the Báb, recognized as the 17th Letter of the Living.
Attends the Conference of Badasht, where she removes her veil, symbolizing a break with the past.
Placed under house arrest due to her influential teachings.
Executed by strangulation in a garden in Tehran for her Bábí faith.

originalEn.quote_en: There is a fire in my heart, with that fire the world burns. The tears flowing from my eyes reveal my secret.
originalEn.meaning_en: This line illustrates how intense inner love (fire) transforms one's perception of the world, and how the resulting pain (tears) reveals one's hidden emotions. It symbolizes the passionate love for God in Sufism.

originalEn.quote_en: Wealth without knowledge is like a wall without a foundation for an ignorant person, and knowledge without wealth is like a body without a soul.
originalEn.meaning_en: Fuzûlî emphasized the balance between knowledge and material means. One is incomplete without the other, conveying the practical wisdom that true value comes from the harmony of both.

originalEn.quote_en: There is nothing as precious as love in the world, for love is the essence of knowledge.
originalEn.meaning_en: This line reveals the core of Fuzûlî's philosophy. For him, love, especially divine love, is the source of all knowledge and understanding, and the ultimate value to be pursued in life.

originalEn.event: Born as Muhammad bin Suleyman, likely in the region of modern-day Iraq (Karbala or Hillah).
Receives a comprehensive education, mastering Azerbaijani, Persian, and Arabic, along with theology and mathematics.
The Ottoman Empire under Suleiman the Magnificent captures Baghdad from the Safavids, shifting the political landscape of Fuzûlî's life.
Composes his masterpiece, the romantic epic 'Leylâ vü Mecnûn' (Layla and Majnun) in Azerbaijani Turkic.
Writes the famous 'Şikâyetnâme' (The Book of Complaint), a satirical prose work protesting his not receiving a promised government stipend.
Dies during a plague outbreak in Karbala, where he was serving as a custodian at the shrine of Imam Husayn.

originalEn.quote_en: I have labored to seek knowledge my whole life, and from my words, you may gather wisdom.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects his belief that the pursuit of knowledge is a lifelong endeavor and that its essence is captured in language and writing, signifying his pride in his life's work, the 'Shahnameh'.

originalEn.quote_en: The heart of a just king is like a shining sun, that warms the entire world.
originalEn.meaning_en: A recurring theme in the 'Shahnameh', this emphasizes that a ruler's most important virtues are fairness and justice, and that such leadership brings prosperity and stability to the people.

originalEn.quote_en: No one will remain in this world forever, except for a good name, which is eternal.
originalEn.meaning_en: This contrasts the transience of material wealth and power with the importance of an eternal reputation earned through good deeds and great works, a sentiment deeply connected to his own life and betrayal by the Sultan.

originalEn.event: Born in Paj, a village near Tus in Khorasan, Sasanian Empire (modern-day Iran).
Begins composing the Shahnameh, his life's work, to preserve the history and mythology of Persia.
Completes the first version of the Shahnameh and presents it to Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni, who famously underpays him.
Dies in his hometown of Tus, reportedly in poverty, but leaving behind a work that would define Persian culture.

originalEn.quote_en: A navigator without knowledge is a ship without a rudder.
originalEn.meaning_en: Emphasizes that in both navigation and life, knowledge and understanding are essential to steer oneself towards a goal.

originalEn.quote_en: The sea holds no secrets for those who do not dare to ask.
originalEn.meaning_en: Encourages a spirit of inquiry and discovery, meaning that one must bravely question and explore to understand the unknown.

originalEn.quote_en: To draw a map is to tell a story of the world as you see it.
originalEn.meaning_en: Suggests that cartography is not just a record of objective facts, but a subjective interpretation that contains the creator's perspective, knowledge, and experience.

originalEn.event: Born Ahmed Muhiddin Piri in Gallipoli, Ottoman Empire.
Begins his seafaring career, sailing with his uncle, the corsair and admiral Kemal Reis.
Compiles his first world map, famously known as the Piri Reis map, and presents it to Sultan Selim I.
Completes his 'Kitab-ı Bahriye' (Book of Navigation), a detailed atlas of the Mediterranean.
Creates his second world map, of which only a fragment showing Central and North America survives.
Appointed as the Reis (Admiral) of the Ottoman Fleet in the Indian Ocean.
Executed in Cairo on charges of treason and bribery following the siege of Hormuz.

originalEn.quote_en: The wisest man is he who sees his own faults, and the most foolish is he who is blind to them.
originalEn.meaning_en: True wisdom comes from self-reflection and humility. Acknowledging and understanding one's own shortcomings is the first step toward improvement.

originalEn.quote_en: I would go even to China in search of knowledge.
originalEn.meaning_en: This expresses a passion for learning that transcends any difficulty or distance. It signifies that the pursuit of knowledge knows no borders.

originalEn.quote_en: To look after the welfare of the people is the most important duty of a ruler.
originalEn.meaning_en: The true strength of a ruler comes not from military might or wealth, but from the responsibility to care for the people and create a just society.

originalEn.event: Born in Rayy (in modern-day Iran).
Becomes the fifth Abbasid Caliph after the death of his brother al-Hadi.
Establishes or greatly expands the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) in Baghdad.
Launches a major campaign against the Byzantine Empire, reaching the Bosporus.
Divides the empire's succession between his sons, al-Amin and al-Ma'mun, setting the stage for future conflict.
Orders the arrest of the powerful Barmakid family, ending their influence over the court.
Dies in Tus (Khorasan, modern-day Iran) while on an expedition to quell a rebellion.

originalEn.quote_en: Fear is the cheapest room in the house. I would like to see you living in better conditions.
originalEn.meaning_en: This quote suggests that fear is a base, constricting emotion that limits our potential. Hafez encourages us to live in higher states of being—love, courage, and trust—reminding us that moving beyond fear is the first step to a more expansive and joyful life.

originalEn.quote_en: I have learned so much from God that I can no longer call myself a Christian, a Hindu, a Muslim, a Buddhist, a Jew.
originalEn.meaning_en: This verse showcases Hafez's universalist spirituality. He transcended the boundaries of religious dogma to see the divine truth at the core of all faiths. For him, a true connection with the divine goes beyond any religious label.

originalEn.quote_en: Cherish this moment. This moment is your life.
originalEn.meaning_en: Reflecting a core tenet of Sufi thought, this quote urges a full engagement with the present. Hafez teaches that life's true essence is not found in regretting the past or worrying about the future, but in the richness and beauty of the now.

originalEn.event: Born Shams-ud-Din Mohammad Hafez-e Shirazi in Shiraz, Persia.
The Muzaffarid dynasty captures Shiraz, impacting Hafez's position and patronage at court.
Shah Shuja, a patron of poetry, restores Hafez to his position at court after a period of disfavor.
Falls from favor again and may have entered a period of exile from Shiraz.
Timur (Tamerlane) conquers Shiraz. A legendary, though likely apocryphal, meeting occurs between the poet and the conqueror.
Dies in Shiraz and is buried in the Musalla Gardens. His tomb is now a national monument and pilgrimage site.

originalEn.quote_en: Make peace with your past so it won't screw up the present.
originalEn.meaning_en: This means focusing on learning from history, not being trapped by it, to build a better future.

originalEn.quote_en: The greatest enemy of the Turk is the Turk who is an enemy of learning and science.
originalEn.meaning_en: This emphasizes that internal ignorance and resistance to progress are more dangerous than any external foe.

originalEn.quote_en: A nation which has chosen to beget a new life is a nation that is not afraid of death.
originalEn.meaning_en: This signifies that true national rebirth requires immense sacrifice and a willingness to face existential threats for the sake of a new beginning.

originalEn.event: Born in Constantinople, Ottoman Empire.
Graduated from the American College for Girls in Constantinople.
Divorced her first husband, Salih Zeki, over his decision to take a second wife.
Delivered her famous speech at the Sultanahmet demonstration against the Allied occupation.
Fled to Anatolia to join the Turkish National Movement; served in the Turkish War of Independence.
Lived in self-imposed exile in Europe and the United States due to political disagreements with the new government.
Returned to Turkey after Atatürk's death.
Elected as a member of the Grand National Assembly of Turkey.
Died in Istanbul.

originalEn.quote_en: An eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth.
originalEn.meaning_en: Known as Lex Talionis, this principle dictates retributive justice where the punishment resembles the injury in kind and degree inflicted upon the victim.

originalEn.quote_en: That the strong might not injure the weak.
originalEn.meaning_en: From the prologue to the Code, this highlights the fundamental purpose of law: to protect the most vulnerable members of society and ensure social order.

originalEn.quote_en: I am the shepherd of the people.
originalEn.meaning_en: This metaphor signifies that a ruler's duty is to care for, guide, and ensure the well-being and prosperity of their subjects as a sacred responsibility.

originalEn.event: Born in Babylon.
Ascends to the throne of Babylon after the abdication of his father, Sin-Muballit.
Conquers the cities of Isin and Uruk.
Defeats a coalition of enemies and conquers the powerful kingdom of Larsa, significantly expanding his empire.
Conquers the kingdom of Mari, securing control over most of Mesopotamia.
Establishes the Code of Hammurabi on a diorite stele, which is publicly displayed in Babylon.
Dies after a reign of 42 years, and is succeeded by his son, Samsu-iluna.

originalEn.quote_en: Where a woman's tear falls, a man will kneel.
originalEn.meaning_en: This symbolizes the emotional power and influence of a woman. I learned that tears were not a sign of weakness, but a potent tool of persuasion that could move even the strongest of men.

originalEn.quote_en: Every flower in the garden of power is fed with blood.
originalEn.meaning_en: This signifies the harsh reality that gaining and maintaining power requires sacrifice and difficult decisions. I had to make hard choices to protect my children and myself.

originalEn.quote_en: A building of stone may fall, but a legacy of knowledge and charity is eternal.
originalEn.meaning_en: This reflects my belief that a true legacy lies not in physical structures but in the positive impact one has on people's lives. The charitable complexes I built are the most important testaments to my power.

originalEn.event: Born as Aleksandra Lisowska in Ruthenia, Kingdom of Poland (modern-day Ukraine).
Captured by Crimean Tatars and brought to Constantinople, entering the Imperial Harem of Sultan Suleiman.
Gives birth to her first son, Şehzade Mehmed, solidifying her status in the harem.
Suleiman frees and legally marries Hurrem, making her the Haseki Sultan, an unprecedented event.
Her rival, Grand Vizier Pargalı Ibrahim Pasha, is executed. Many historians believe she influenced this decision.
Begins construction of the Haseki Sultan Complex in Constantinople, a major charitable foundation.
Suleiman's firstborn son, Şehzade Mustafa, is executed on suspicion of treason. Hurrem is widely seen as having orchestrated his downfall to secure the throne for her own sons.
Dies in the Topkapi Palace and is buried in a mausoleum next to Suleiman's at the Süleymaniye Mosque.

originalEn.quote_en: All men dream, but not equally... the dreamers of the day are dangerous men, for they may act on their dreams with open eyes, to make them possible.
originalEn.meaning_en: This highlights the difference between passive wishing and active creation. True change is brought about not by idle fantasy, but by those who act on their will in the real world to make things happen.

originalEn.quote_en: I loved you, so I drew these tides of men into my hands and wrote my will across the sky in stars...
originalEn.meaning_en: A poetic expression of his personal dedication and ambition for the Arab cause. It shows his actions were driven not just by military duty, but by a profound personal motivation.

originalEn.quote_en: The printing press is the greatest weapon in the armoury of the modern commander.
originalEn.meaning_en: This shows his belief in the power of ideas, propaganda, and communication in modern warfare and politics. He understood that controlling information and public opinion was as crucial as physical force.

originalEn.event: Born Thomas Edward Lawrence in Tremadog, Wales.
Studies Modern History at Jesus College, Oxford, and undertakes a walking tour of Syria to research crusader castles for his thesis.
World War I begins; he is assigned to the geographical section of the General Staff in London and later to military intelligence in Cairo.
Joins the Arab Revolt as a British liaison officer to the forces of Emir Faisal.
Leads a small Arab force in a daring desert march to capture the strategic Ottoman-held port of Aqaba.
Participates in the capture of Damascus, a key victory for the Allied and Arab forces.
Serves as an advisor to Emir Faisal's Arab delegation at the Paris Peace Conference, where he is disillusioned by the outcome.
Enlists in the Royal Air Force under the assumed name John Hume Ross to escape his fame.
Publishes a private, subscribers-only edition of his epic war memoir, 'Seven Pillars of Wisdom'.
Dies at the age of 46 from injuries sustained in a motorcycle accident in Dorset, England.

originalEn:
Mustafa Kemal Atatürk was a visionary statesman, military leader, and the founder of the Republic of Turkey, serving as its first president from 1923 until his death in 1938. Emerging as a brilliant military strategist during World War I, he famously led the Ottoman forces to a pivotal victory at the Battle of Gallipoli. However, it was the aftermath of the war that revealed his true greatness, as he successfully led the Turkish National Movement in the War of Independence, ultimately saving the nation from partition by the Allied powers.

Following the establishment of the republic, Atatürk embarked on a sweeping and unprecedented series of political, economic, and cultural reforms designed to transform the new nation into a modern, secular, and progressive state. He abolished the centuries-old Ottoman Caliphate, introduced a new alphabet based on Latin letters, revolutionized the legal system inspired by European models, and significantly advanced women's rights, including granting them the right to vote. Through his unwavering determination, Atatürk reshaped the destiny of his people and forged a lasting legacy that continues to define the Turkish identity.

Following the devastating defeat of the Ottoman Empire in World War I, Mustafa Kemal Atatürk faced the seemingly insurmountable task of rescuing a fractured and occupied nation. The Treaty of Sèvres in 1920 effectively partitioned the Ottoman lands, handing control of significant territories to the victorious Allied powers, including Britain, France, Italy, and Greece. Atatürk had to contend not only with superior foreign armies encroaching on all fronts but also with a compliant Ottoman government in Istanbul that had capitulated to the occupiers, branding him a rebel and sentencing him to death in absentia.

His struggle extended beyond the battlefield; he faced the monumental challenge of modernizing an exhausted, impoverished, and deeply traditional society. After securing the nation's independence, he encountered fierce resistance from conservative religious factions, entrenched political elites, and widespread illiteracy as he sought to dismantle centuries-old institutions like the Sultanate and the Caliphate to build a secular, modern republic from the ashes of a fallen empire.

Confronting the imminent dismemberment of his country, Atatürk rallied the demoralized Turkish populace by igniting a fierce sense of national unity and purpose. He organized a grassroots national resistance movement, establishing the Grand National Assembly in Ankara to serve as a legitimate counter-government to the one in Istanbul. Through brilliant military strategy and unyielding resolve, he led the Turkish National Movement to decisive victories in the Turkish War of Independence, successfully repelling the Greek, Armenian, and French forces and forcing the Allies to negotiate the much more favorable Treaty of Lausanne in 1923, which internationally recognized the borders of the new Republic of Turkey.

To overcome the immense social and cultural stagnation of the newly formed republic, Atatürk launched a sweeping series of radical reforms with breathtaking speed and determination. He abolished the Sultanate and Caliphate, completely secularizing the state and the legal system. By introducing a new Latin-based Turkish alphabet, expanding public education, and championing women's rights—including the right to vote and hold public office—he effectively dismantled the remnants of the Ottoman past, forcefully dragging his nation into the modern era and laying the unbreakable foundation for contemporary Turkey.

Atatürk’s core philosophical legacy, known as Kemalism, is anchored in the uncompromising belief that rationalism, science, and secularism are the essential pillars of a modern, prosperous society. He famously proclaimed, "Peace at home, peace in the world," championing the idea that a nation's strength and sovereignty depend not on imperial conquest, but on internal education, relentless modernization, and the steadfast separation of religion from the affairs of the state.

originalEn.event: 오스만 제국의 지배 하에 있던 셀라닉주(지금의 그리스 테살로니키)에서 태어났다.
살로니카 군사 학교에 입학하였다.
마나스트르 군사 고등학교에 입학했다.
오스만제국 군사학교에 입학했다.
오스만제국 군사학교를 졸업했다.
오스만제국 군사대학을 졸업했다.
시니어 캡틴으로 진급했다.
마나스트르에 위치한 3군 사령부로 전속되었다.
동부 루멜리아의 오스만 제국 철도 감독관으로 임명됐다.
청년 튀르크 혁명에 가담했다.
부족 반란 진압 후 이스탄불에 다시 돌아왔다.
이스탄불에서 발생한 반혁명 운동인 3월 31일 사건 진압에 기여했다.
오스만 제국령 알바니아로 발령받았다.
오스만 제국의 관전장교 신분으로 프랑스 피카르디 대규모 훈련에 참여했다.
이스탄불에서 오스만 제국 전쟁부에서 잠시 복무했다.
오스만령 트리폴리타니아(현재의 리비아)에 발령받아 이탈리아-튀르크 전쟁에 참전했다.
토브루크 전투에서 대승을 거두었다.
데르나 전투에서 왼쪽 눈에 부상을 입어 시력 손상을 입었다.
데르나의 오스만 제국군의 지휘관이 됐다.
발칸 전쟁이 발발하자 이탈리아-튀르크 전쟁 전선에서 귀환했다.
이탈리아-튀르크 전쟁이 끝났고, 우시 조약으로 오스만령 트리폴리타니아 등을 이탈리아 왕국에 넘겨줬다.
사카르야 전투에서 크게 승리를 거두어 대그리스주의를 끝내게 만들었다.
그리스와의 전쟁에서 대승을 거둔 날로, 터키의 국경일로 기념되고 있다.
술탄제를 폐지하고 메흐메트 6세를 폐위했다.
연합국과 새로운 조약(로잔 조약)을 체결하였다.
이스탄불에서 앙카라로 천도하고 공화제를 선포하였으며, 대통령에 취임했다.
632년 이래 1300년 동안 이어져 오던 칼리프제를 폐지하였다.
복장 개혁을 시행하여 여성들의 복장을 해방시키고, 남녀평등교육을 시행하였으며, 이슬람력을 유럽식 그레고리력으로 대체했다.
민법을 개정해 일부다처제를 금지하고 일부일처제를 확립하였다.
튀르키예어의 아랍 문자 표기법을 폐기하고 로마자 표기법으로 변경하였다.
여성에게도 선거권을 부여하였다.
튀르키예 의회가 '튀르크의 아버지'라는 뜻의 '아타튀르크' 칭호를 헌정하였다.
아타튀르크의 건강이 악화되는 징후들이 나타나기 시작했다.
얄로바로의 여행 중에 심각한 병으로 고통을 받았고, 간경화증 진단을 받았다.
돌마바흐체 궁전에서 57세의 나이로 사망했다.

originalEn:
Rosa Parks was a fearless civil rights activist whose quiet but powerful act of defiance profoundly altered the course of American history. On December 1, 1955, in Montgomery, Alabama, she famously refused to give up her bus seat to a white passenger, a courageous stand against the oppressive and deeply entrenched racial segregation laws of the Jim Crow era. Her arrest sparked the Montgomery Bus Boycott, a pivotal 381-day mass protest that galvanized the African American community and brought national attention to the injustices of segregation.

Often called the 'mother of the civil rights movement,' Parks's singular act of courage inspired countless others to join the fight for equality and civil rights in the United States. She worked closely with prominent leaders like Martin Luther King Jr., and her lifelong dedication to the struggle for racial equality left an indelible mark on the world. Her legacy remains a powerful testament to how an ordinary individual's commitment to justice can ignite a movement that challenges systemic oppression and changes society for the better.

In the deeply segregated American South of the 1950s, Rosa Parks faced the pervasive and crushing reality of Jim Crow laws, which institutionalized racial discrimination and subjugated African Americans in nearly every aspect of daily life. As a dedicated activist and the secretary of the Montgomery chapter of the NAACP, she constantly confronted the daily humiliations and systemic violence of a society that aggressively fought to maintain white supremacy. Her activism routinely put her personal safety and livelihood at profound risk in an environment where resistance to segregation was often met with brutal retaliation.

Her greatest trial crystallized on December 1, 1955, when she was ordered to give up her seat to a white passenger on a Montgomery, Alabama, city bus. By making the conscious, incredibly dangerous decision to refuse, she subjected herself to immediate arrest, a criminal conviction, and intense public backlash. In the aftermath of her arrest, she and her husband faced relentless harassment, death threats, and the loss of their jobs, enduring severe economic and emotional hardship as the struggle for civil rights escalated around them.

Rosa Parks overcame these monumental injustices through a quiet but unshakeable defiance rooted in deep moral conviction and years of strategic grassroots organizing. Her arrest was not merely an isolated act of fatigue, but a calculated stand by a seasoned activist ready to challenge an unjust system. By offering herself as the catalyst, she ignited the Montgomery Bus Boycott, a massive, 381-day coordinated protest led by a young Martin Luther King Jr., which united the local African American community in walking, carpooling, and enduring tremendous hardship rather than riding segregated buses.

Through her unwavering stoicism and dignity in the face of legal persecution and severe personal costs, Parks became an indestructible symbol of the civil rights movement. Her bravery ultimately paid off when the boycott culminated in the landmark 1956 Supreme Court ruling that declared segregated seating on public buses unconstitutional. Despite being forced to relocate to Detroit due to ongoing threats and blacklisting, she continued her tireless advocacy for racial equality and social justice until the end of her life, proving that ordinary individuals can dismantle extraordinary oppression.

Rosa Parks’s core philosophical legacy rests on the profound impact of principled, peaceful resistance against systemic injustice. She demonstrated that true power often lies not in loud rhetoric, but in the quiet, fearless refusal to comply with indignity, teaching the world that "you must never be fearful about what you are doing when it is right."

originalEn.event: 앨라배마주 터스키지에서 로자 루이즈 맥콜리(Rosa Louise McCauley)로 태어남.
레이먼드 파크스와 결혼함.
남편의 외조로 고등학교 교육을 마침.
전미 흑인 지위 향상 협회(NAACP) 몽고메리 지부에 가입하며 민권운동에 참여하기 시작함.
앨라배마주 몽고메리에서 백인 승객에게 자리를 양보하라는 버스 운전사의 지시를 거부하여 경찰에 체포됨.
몽고메리 버스 보이콧 운동에 각지의 흑인 교회가 동참한다고 발표함.
질서를 어지럽힌 행동을 한 혐의로 기소되어 유죄를 선고받고 벌금과 법정비용을 물게 됨.
시온 교회에서 '몽고메리 진보 협회'를 결성하고 마틴 루터 킹 주니어 박사를 회장으로 선출함.
NAACP 몽고메리 지부 서기 활동을 마침.
버스 사건을 알리기 위해 각지를 돌며 연설하기 시작함.
아프리카계 미국 하원의원 존 코니어스의 비서로 고용되어 디트로이트 사무실에서 일하게 됨.
비서직에서 은퇴함.
자서전 《로자 파크스: 나의 이야기》를 짐 해스킨스와 공동 집필함.
회고록 《조용한 힘 : 나라를 바꾼 여성의 신념, 소망, 심장》을 그레고리 J. 리드와 공동 집필함.
빌 클린턴 행정부로부터 민간인에게 수여하는 최고의 훈장인 대통령 자유 훈장을 수여받음.
92세로 디트로이트에서 사망함.

originalEn:
Simone de Beauvoir was a pioneering French existentialist philosopher, writer, and feminist thinker whose profound intellectual contributions challenged societal norms and reshaped modern feminist theory. She is best known for her groundbreaking 1949 work, 'The Second Sex,' an extensive and meticulously argued treatise that dismantled the historical, social, and cultural constructs defining womanhood. By declaring that 'one is not born, but rather becomes, a woman,' Beauvoir boldly exposed the mechanisms of patriarchal oppression and laid the indispensable foundation for the second-wave feminism movement.

Beyond her monumental feminist literature, Beauvoir's life and work were deeply intertwined with the development of existentialism, closely collaborating with her lifelong companion Jean-Paul Sartre while maintaining her distinct philosophical voice. Through her novels, essays, and autobiographical volumes, she fiercely advocated for authentic living, individual freedom, and ethical responsibility in a complex world. Her enduring intellectual courage and incisive critique of gender inequality continue to inspire generations of thinkers and activists striving for true liberation and equality.

As a female philosopher and intellectual in early to mid-20th-century France, Simone de Beauvoir faced immense societal prejudice and intellectual marginalization. The publication of her groundbreaking work, *The Second Sex* (1949), brought intense backlash from both the conservative establishment and the Vatican, which placed the book on its Index of Forbidden Books. She was frequently attacked personally, dismissed by male peers, and her philosophical contributions were often unfairly overshadowed by her relationship with Jean-Paul Sartre.

De Beauvoir overcame these trials through unyielding intellectual courage and an unwavering commitment to her existentialist principles. Rather than silencing her, the fierce criticism fueled her determination to expose the systemic mechanisms of patriarchal oppression. She continued to write prolifically across various genres—including philosophy, fiction, and essays—solidifying her independent voice. By rigorously dissecting the social construction of gender, she laid the foundational theories for the modern feminist movement, refusing to be defined by anyone but herself.

"One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman." True freedom is achieved only when one takes absolute responsibility for defining their own existence, stripping away the artificial labels imposed by society to forge an authentic life.

originalEn.event: 프랑스 파리에서 시몬 드 보부아르 출생.
소르본에서 라이프니츠에 대해 발표하고, 철학 교수자격시험에서 2등으로 통과.
소설 《초대받은 여자》(L'Invitée)를 출판.
실존주의 윤리학에 대한 논문 《피루스와 키네아스》(Pyrrhus et Cinéas)를 저술.
《애매함의 도덕에 관하여》(Pour Une Morale de L'ambiguïté)를 출판.
《제2의 성》(Le Deuxième Sexe)이 프랑스에서 출판.
자서전 《처녀 시절》(Mémoires d'une jeune fille rangée)을 출판.
자서전 《나이의 힘》(La Force de l'âge)을 출판.
자서전 《사물의 힘》(La Force des choses)을 출판.
산문 《노년》(La Vieillesse)을 저술.
343선언에 서명하며 낙태 허용을 주장.
자서전 《결국》(Tout compte fait)을 출판.
어린 시절 여성으로서 겪었던 이야기들을 수록한 "Quand prime le spirituel"을 출판.
사르트르의 말년을 다룬 《작별의 의식》(La Cérémonie Des Adieux)을 저술.
폐렴으로 사망하여 파리 몽파르나스 묘지에 안장.

originalEn:
Hannah Arendt stands as a towering intellectual figure of the twentieth century, casting an unflinching gaze upon the darkest chapters of human history. Through her rigorous philosophical inquiry, she illuminated the nature of power, authority, and the roots of totalitarianism. Her courageous exploration of the 'banality of evil' challenged conventional morality, demanding that individuals confront the profound responsibility of active, critical thought in the face of systemic tyranny.

Her legacy is not merely one of historical analysis, but an urgent call to the active life—the *vita activa*. Arendt envisioned a public sphere where freedom is realized through collective action and the fearless exchange of ideas. In a world continually threatened by political alienation and ideological conformity, her majestic body of work remains an essential beacon, guiding us toward the enduring promise of democratic engagement and the preservation of human dignity.

As a German-Jewish political theorist born in 1906, Hannah Arendt faced the horrifying rise of totalitarianism firsthand. Fleeing Nazi Germany in 1933 after being briefly arrested by the Gestapo, she became a stateless refugee for nearly two decades, enduring profound dislocation and the loss of her homeland. Later, her coverage of the Adolf Eichmann trial in 1961, where she coined the phrase "the banality of evil," brought intense controversy and bitter condemnation from many within the Jewish community, causing her deep personal pain and isolating her from former friends.

Arendt navigated these profound traumas by dedicating her life to understanding the dark political realities of her time, transforming her experience of statelessness into profound political theory. Instead of succumbing to despair or bitterness, she wrote seminal works like *The Origins of Totalitarianism* to dissect the mechanisms of terror. During the Eichmann controversy, she steadfastly defended her philosophical integrity, maintaining that facing uncomfortable truths about human nature and blind obedience was essential to preventing future atrocities.

True political freedom and moral responsibility require the continuous exercise of independent thought. The greatest evil is not necessarily committed by sociopaths, but by ordinary people who surrender their capacity to think and judge for themselves.

originalEn:
Agatha Christie, the undisputed Queen of Crime, wove unparalleled tapestries of mystery and suspense that have captivated the world for over a century. With an extraordinarily sharp intellect and an uncanny understanding of human psychology, she transformed the classic whodunit into a profound exploration of human nature. Her legendary detectives, from the meticulous Hercule Poirot to the astute Miss Marple, navigate webs of deception and hidden motives, ensuring that justice prevails in even the most perplexing circumstances.

Beyond her astonishing commercial success, Christie's enduring brilliance lies in her masterful narrative architecture and her ability to misdirect and enthrall the reader. She constructed elegant puzzles that are as psychologically complex as they are delightfully entertaining. Today, her majestic catalog of literature stands as a timeless monument to the power of a brilliantly crafted story, cementing her legacy as an immortal titan of modern fiction.

Agatha Christie's life was marked by severe personal and professional crises, most notably in 1926. In that year, her mother, to whom she was deeply attached, passed away. Shortly thereafter, her husband Archie Christie demanded a divorce, revealing his affair with another woman. This convergence of grief and betrayal culminated in her famous 11-day disappearance, a psychological breaking point that sparked a nationwide manhunt and intense media scrutiny. Moreover, as a female author, she initially struggled against an industry that often underestimated women writers.

Christie overcame her devastating personal crisis by channeling her resilience back into her writing and boldly rebuilding her life. Following her divorce, she traveled alone on the Orient Express to the Middle East, a courageous act of independence that opened a vibrant new chapter in her life and heavily inspired her future masterpieces. She relentlessly honed her craft, constructing intricate puzzles that revolutionized the detective genre. Through sheer discipline and prolific output, she transcended the scandal to become the best-selling fiction writer of all time.

Human nature is universally flawed, and evil can lurk behind the most respectable facades. Justice and truth can always be uncovered through meticulous observation, patience, and the unwavering application of human logic.

originalEn.event: 영국 데번주 토키에서 태어남.
16세 때 노래와 피아노를 배우기 위해 파리로 감.
영국 항공대 대령 아치벌드 크리스티와 첫 결혼을 함.
제1차 세계 대전 동안 병원 약국에서 일함.
외동딸 로절린드 힉스가 태어남.
12월 8일, 10일간 행방불명되어 언론의 주목을 받음.
고고학자 맥스 맬로원과 재혼함.
대영 제국 훈장 3등급(CBE)을 받음.
대영 제국 훈장 2등급(DBE)을 받음.
85세의 나이로 사망함.

originalEn:
Queen Elizabeth I emerged as a monumental sovereign who navigated the treacherous waters of sixteenth-century European politics with unmatched grace and strategic genius. Ascending to a fractured realm, she forged the Golden Age of England, uniting a divided nation and transforming it into a formidable global power. Her reign was a magnificent symphony of cultural renaissance and political stability, defined by a fierce independence that successfully defied the greatest empires of her time.

She was the Virgin Queen, married only to her country, embodying the ultimate sacrifice of personal desire for the glory of the state. Under her majestic vision, English literature flourished, and bold explorers expanded the horizons of the known world. Her indomitable spirit and masterful diplomacy cemented her legacy as one of history's most legendary monarchs, whose enduring mythos continues to inspire an eternal reverence for female leadership and resilience.

Queen Elizabeth I's path to the throne and her subsequent reign were fraught with mortal peril and immense instability. Declared illegitimate after the execution of her mother, Anne Boleyn, she spent her youth navigating deadly political and religious machinations, even being imprisoned in the Tower of London by her half-sister Mary I under suspicion of treason. Once crowned, she faced a deeply fractured nation torn by religious strife, continuous assassination plots, relentless pressure to marry to secure an heir, and the existential threat of foreign invasion, culminating in the Spanish Armada of 1588.

Elizabeth triumphed by wielding supreme political acumen, pragmatic compromise, and masterful public relations. She established the Elizabethan Religious Settlement to soothe domestic religious tensions and brilliantly used her unmarried status—the "Virgin Queen" persona—as a diplomatic bargaining chip and a symbol of her exclusive marriage to England. During the Spanish Armada crisis, she inspired her troops with iconic rhetoric, declaring she had the "heart and stomach of a king." Her cautious, calculating leadership ushered in a golden age of stability and cultural flourishing.

True sovereignty requires the subordination of personal desires to the duty of the state. Strength lies not in absolute dominance, but in the pragmatic balance of opposing forces and the enduring devotion to one's people.
